      ANTH 249-01 (10798)The Middle East Through FilmMeeting: M 7:00 - 10:00 pmCARN 06AInstructor: Jenna Rice Rahaim020

      Cross-listed with INTL 258-01 (10881) and MCST 258-01 (10882)

      > Details for ANTH 249-01

      This course provides an introduction to Middle Eastern history, politics, and cultural life through the lens of film. Over the course of the semester, we will examine the development of both local and regional cinematic traditions; the role of film in the production of collective memory of conflict and war; techniques and visual rhetorics of ethnographic film; and the power relations inherent in the cinematic gaze. Readings will offer an introduction to methods of film analysis as well as to the political and artistic history of the modern Middle East.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      ANTH 249-01 course materials and textbooks
      ANTH 251-F1 (10009)Politics of Memory in Latin AmericaMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmTHEATR 206Instructor: Olga González-116

      First-Year Course Only; cross-listed with LATI 251-F1 (10010)

      > Details for ANTH 251-F1

      This course examines and critically analyzes various approaches to the study of how different individuals and communities in particular historical and cultural scenarios in contemporary Latin America create meanings about their past experience with political violence. The course addresses questions related to the tension between remembering and forgetting, the presence of conflicting memories and truths and how these are negotiated or not through distinct forms of representation. The cultural analysis of different means of representation: human rights and truth commission reports, testimonials, film, art and memorials will be the basis for class discussions on different notions of truth and different forms of truth-telling. A close examination of these forms of representation will reveal the extent to which they can conflict with each other while at the same time feed on each other, creating "effects of truth" and leaving room for secrecy as a mode of truth-telling. Finally, the course will also compel students to think about what consequences the politics of memory have for the future.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ANTH 251-F1 course materials and textbooks
      ANTH 294-01 (10011)Indigenous Peoples in the Asia-Pacific: Environment, Society, and PoliticsMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 112Instructor: Arjun Guneratne
      Tom Robertson      		2330

      Cross-listed with ENVI 294-04 (10012) and ASIA 294-02 (10894)

      > Details for ANTH 294-01

      Asia and the Pacific Islands host more of the world’s Indigenous people than any other continent -- roughly 260 million out of 370 million worldwide (70 percent). In addition, Indigenous people in Asia generally make up the continent’s “poorest of the poor,” facing systemic disadvantage including limited access to healthcare, education, land, capital, and labor, as well as weaker political representation. In this course, we examine the lived experience of indigenous people in the Asia-Pacific through three analytical frames: their relationship to the state and the global order, their relationship to territory and the environment, and challenges to cultural survival. Along the way, we discuss colonialism and settler colonialism, colonial and contemporary representations of indigenous people, the challenges to both cultural and political survival of indigenous people posed by state formation, the challenges of resource extraction and globalization, conservation as a form of colonialism, and the creation of an indigenous diaspora. The course includes guest speakers and field trips.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ANTH 294-01 course materials and textbooks
      ANTH 294-02 (10790)Digital Cultural HeritageMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmLIBR 250Instructor: Aisling Quigley916

      > Details for ANTH 294-02

      Cultural heritage sites, including libraries, archives and museums, have existed in some shape or form for a very long time. Computers, on the other hand, have only been a part of these institutions for about sixty years. Although technologies offer more efficient and cost-effective ways to store and disseminate information and promise greater accessibility to materials, that doesn’t necessarily mean that they successfully facilitate the missions of these cultural heritage sites or the needs of their visitors. Why is this the case? Do digital technologies truly have the potential to decentralize and democratize these spaces? What can they tell us about what we value, as a culture? In this interdisciplinary course, we will reflect on the impact of digital technologies on cultural heritage sites, and museums in particular, starting in the 1960s and continuing through the present. Interwoven throughout the course, we will discuss decolonization, digital repatriation, obsolescence, cultural heritage and AI, and collective remembering. The course culminates in the creation of an online exhibition.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      Course Materials:

      ANTH 294-02 course materials and textbooks
      ANTH 394-02 (10797)Religion and ColonialismMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmCARN 06AInstructor: Robert Weiner1120

      > Details for ANTH 394-02

      This course examines the transformation of Indigenous religious systems in response to European colonial expansion and how radically new religious movements grow out of spiritual crises of conquest. We will study various types of prophetic traditions, revolutionary ideologies, and revitalization movements that Indigenous peoples developed to preserve their cultural identities in the face of forced integration into imperial systems. After problematizing the very notion of “Indigenous religions,” the course explores how traditional spiritual leaders attempted to explain and resist colonial presence through novel systems of belief, material culture, and ritual practice. Drawing on global case studies, we will focus on religious revolutions spurred by European expansion in North America, Mesoamerica, Africa, and Oceania. Key case studies include: Aztec responses to Spanish conquest, the Pueblo Revolt, the Ghost Dance, Haudenosaunee religious revitalization, the 1920s Pueblo Dance controversy, Melanesian cargo cults, the Great Xhosa Cattle-Killing, and Christianity as a Jewish response to Roman imperialism. Contemporary Indigenous traditions of activism and protest against extractive industries, such as resistance to the Dakota Access Pipeline and fracking near Chaco Canyon, will serve as a final focus of the course.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      Course Materials:

      ANTH 394-02 course materials and textbooks
      ANTH 487-01 (10013)Theory in AnthropologyMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amCARN 05Instructor: Olga González520

      > Details for ANTH 487-01

      This course introduces students to the broad range of explanations for social and cultural phenomena used by anthropologists since the emergence of the discipline in the 19th century. The course focuses on the development of three broad theoretical approaches: The American school of cultural anthropology, British social anthropology, and the French school that emerged from the work of Durkheim and his followers. The course also examines theoretical approaches such as cultural materialism, and symbolic and interpretive approaches to the study of culture. Prerequisite(s): Junior or senior standing.  Students should have at least two courses in anthropology including ANTH 101 or ANTH 111, or the permission of the instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ANTH 487-01 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Art and Art History

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      ART 130-01 (10027)Drawing IMeeting: T R 1:20 - 4:30 pmART 302Instructor: Nicole Simpkins215

      Seats saved for: 4 juniors, 4 sophomores, 4 incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 130-01

      This course is an introduction to fundamental drawing materials and techniques. Students explore a variety of themes and subjects, including still life, architecture, figure drawing, portraiture, and imagination. Formal elements covered include: line, value, volume, space, proportion, perspective, mark-making, and composition. Context for assignments is given through frequent discussion of both historical and contemporary artworks. In group critiques and discussions, we consider composition, representational accuracy, creative expression, content, and intention.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 130-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 131-01 (10028)Ceramics IMeeting: T R 8:00 - 11:10 amART 113Instructor: Summer Hills-Bonczyk014

      Seats saved for: 6 seniors, 4 juniors, 2 sophomores, 2 incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 131-01

      This introductory course provides a supportive studio environment for the exploration of diverse approaches to the Ceramic Art field. Four studio projects cover basic handbuilding and wheelthrowing techniques with room for individual growth and creativity. Emphasis is placed on development of strong three-dimensional forms and surface decoration. Thematic assignments and guided ideation processes are offered to support creative problem solving. This course aims to promote an appreciation for the spirit of handmade objects. Students are introduced to historical and contemporary examples of Ceramic Art that serve as inspiration for creating original work in clay. Discussions, demonstrations, group critiques and gallery/museum visits supplement studio work and introduce students to the broader worlds of art and clay in the Twin Cities and beyond. Learning will be assessed primarily through portfolio production and review, self-assessment and class participation.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 131-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 149-01 (10029)Introduction to Visual CultureMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amARTCOM 102Instructor: Kari Shepherdson-Scott1925

      > Details for ART 149-01

      This course examines material and symbolic practices through myriad visual culture forms, from standards of fine art such as painting and sculpture to mass media including TV, film, advertising, and the Internet. Students will learn different theoretical paradigms and techniques for visual analysis in order to understand how visual culture mediates numerous social, economic, cultural and political relationships. We will investigate these diverse practices through lectures, guest speakers, film, historical art and media and, of course, those proliferating images that define our daily experiences. Offered every Fall.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ART 149-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 160-01 (10030)Introduction to Art History I: From Prehistory to the Medieval PeriodMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amARTCOM 102Instructor: Serdar Yalçin1525

      Cross-listed with CLAS 260-01 (10031)

      > Details for ART 160-01

      ​This course surveys the visual and material culture of Europe, the Middle East and the broader Mediterranean world from prehistory through the late Medieval period, including early Christian, Jewish and Islamic cultures. We consider the artistic and archaeological remains from the Near East, Egypt, the Aegean, Greece, Etruria, Rome, and western Europe from a contextual perspective in order to understand each culture’s ideologies, and the diverse social and artistic purposes for which art and architecture was created and used.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 160-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 194-01 (10770)Leaves to LandscapesMeeting: M W 1:10 - 4:20 pmART 302Instructor: Megan Vossler
      Mary Heskel      		Closed 012

      Cross-listed with BIOL 194-01 (10771); Seniors restricted to 5 seats

      > Details for ART 194-01

      This interdisciplinary course explores the intersection of biology and art, by combining the study of plant ecology with a range of studio art practices. Students will investigate how plants respond to the environment, while developing skills in observational drawing, botanical illustration, visual journaling, and uncovering the uses of plants for pigments and drawing material. In addition, we will learn about plant form, function, physiology, and ecology across a range of environments, explore the history of artmaking as a type of scientific inquiry, and spend plenty of time outdoors. Fieldwork sessions will allow students to observe seasonal changes in plants, and the diversity of plant adaptations, while laboratory and studio sessions will facilitate experimentation and creativity in translating these observations into visual form. No prior drawing or biology experience is required, but an openness to integrating science and art is essential.

      Course Materials:

      ART 194-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 210-01 (10032)Globalization and Contemporary ArtMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amARTCOM 102Instructor: Joanna Inglot516

      Cross-listed with INTL 210-01 (10033)

      > Details for ART 210-01

      This course will examine the developments of contemporary art beyond traditional centers of gravity in Europe and the United States. Using a series of case studies, it will examine how globalization impacts artistic production in different parts of the world, including Asia, Africa, India, Latin America, Russia, Eastern Europe, Russia, and the Middle East. While analyzing a diverse range of artistic practices in these regions, the course will critically explore key discourses around the topic of globalization, including hybridity and diaspora, from post-modern and post-colonial perspectives. No background in art history necessary.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism

      Course Materials:

      ART 210-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 216-01 (10034)Graphic NovelMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amARTCOM 202Instructor: Megan Vossler
      Matt Burgess      		818

      Cross-listed with ENGL 216-01 (10035)

      > Details for ART 216-01

      In this creative writing/studio art hybrid course, students will contribute to the rich medium of graphic novels by writing, drawing, and inking their own original comic book story. The stories will then be mass-produced into individual zines, and collected into a class-wide graphic novel anthology and ebook. This course has two fundamental goals: To help students work individually on a graphic storytelling project of their own devising: from the cultivation of an initial idea, to the further exploration of that idea through multiple drafts and storyboards, and then finally to sending it out into the world as a "completed" project, which is to say a project that is "good enough for now." Our second, equally important goal, is to work collectively to build a mutually supportive artistic community dedicated to helping everyone achieve their best work. Along the way, students will also strengthen their technical drawing and design skills, view and analyze examples of visual storytelling from diverse artists, investigate a range of storytelling structures, learn how to communicate more effectively through images, and gain hands-on experience in the publishing process.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 216-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 233-01 (10036)Photography IMeeting: M W 1:10 - 4:20 pmART 301Instructor: Mara Duvra514

      Seats saved for: 4 seniors, 4 juniors, 3 sophomores, 3 incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 233-01

      This new version of Photo 1 will introduce the conceptual, technical, and historical aspects of photography while focusing on subject matter development, visual narratives, and sequencing with the intent of using the format of the photobook as a method of arranging images and storytelling. This course introduces students to digital workflow, handmade books, and how to take their images from screen to print. Students will learn foundational aspects of digital photography from manual camera operation, lighting, editing, and printing with Adobe Lightroom, Photoshop and InDesign. Presentations, assigned readings, independent research, and group critiques will help students develop the critical and creative skills needed to understand how image making and visual storytelling with sequencing can expand the creative possibilities within photography.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 233-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 234-01 (10037)Painting IMeeting: M W 1:10 - 4:20 pmART 308Instructor: Chris Willcox315

      Seats saved for: 4 seniors, 4 juniors, 4 sophomores, 3 incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 234-01

      An introduction to the studio practice of painting, using oil paint on a variety of supports ranging from paper, board and canvas to non-traditional painting surfaces. Exploration of the practical techniques and mechanics of painting as well as a consideration of content and meaning will be studied. Topics that will be studied include: paint-mixing, color matching, composition, design and a variety of painting styles. Readings and class discussions of historical and contemporary painting practices and issues will develop a visual vocabulary as well as critical/theoretical knowledge to complement technical skills. Lectures, demonstrations, critiques and a gallery/museum visit will supplement studio work.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 234-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 235-01 (10038)Sculpture IMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 11:40 amART 118Instructor: Lela Pierce715

      Seats saved for: 4 seniors, 4 juniors, 4 sophomores, 3 incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 235-01

      This hands-on studio class serves as an introduction to the rich and diverse field of inquiry we call sculpture. Over the course of the semester we will investigate a variety of concepts, materials, and fabrication methods employed in the realization of sculptural works. Examples of fabrication methods may include basic woodworking techniques, carving, basic sewing techniques, "non-traditional" techniques, and others. The early part of the semester is devoted to brief exercises that introduce materials, processes, techniques, tools/equipment, and ways of thinking/unthinking. The latter part of the semester is devoted to fully realized individual projects. Final projects are self-assigned and self-directed via prompts. In addition to the formal and spatial aspects of the sculptures we create, we will consider their symbolic, sociopolitical, ethical, material, and tactile dimensions. Slide presentations, short readings, critiques, group discussions, and gallery/museum visits supplement our studio research. The goal of this class is to conduct a serious and sustained - yet playful - inquiry into the question of sculpture while nurturing individual expression. This class is appropriate for any and all curious students interested in play, thinking with the hands, and reflecting on the results.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 235-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 236-01 (10039)Printmaking IMeeting: T R 1:20 - 4:30 pmART 214Instructor: Ruthann Godollei215

      2 seats saved for incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 236-01

      A hands-on introduction to original printmaking in the media of relief, intaglio, lithography and handset type. Included are discussions of esthetics, the impact of printed imagery on society, the political power of prints, printmaking practices from diverse cultures and from around the world. Learn hand printing technologies as well as use of printing presses and tools in the service of developing your ideas in printed form. Trips to local exhibits and museums frame contemporary and historic understanding of the field. Emphasis is on personal expression and appropriate techniques for the content. Students edition one print at the end of the semester to trade in a portfolio with classmates. Contact the instructor directly for more information.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 236-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 237-01 (10040)Foundations of Ceramics Art: Pottery and PurposeMeeting: T R 1:20 - 4:30 pmART 113Instructor: Summer Hills-Bonczyk512

      Seats saved for: 5 seniors, 3 juniors, 2 sophomores, 2 incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 237-01

      This hands-on studio class will introduce students to the practice of making meaningful pottery for everyday use. Technical demonstrations will introduce ceramic handbuilding and wheel throwing techniques, surface decoration and firing. Lectures, discussions, field trips and readings will provide further context - framing pottery as a relevant, intersectional artform and a catalyst for social connection, cultural affirmation and community care. Emphasis will be placed on a model of the studio as an ecosystem; a collaborative, generative space that enhances human connection and relationship to the natural world. Learning will be assessed primarily through guided self-assessment and class participation. No prerequisite.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 237-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 239-01 (10041)2-D DesignMeeting: T R 8:00 - 11:10 amART 301Instructor: Mara Duvra614

      Seats saved for: 4 seniors, 4 juniors, 3 sophomores, 3 incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 239-01

      This course introduces the conceptual, technical, and historical aspects of graphic design within a fine arts context. Using the foundational Principles and Elements of Art and Design students will learn how to blend personal creativity with technical skills to make image and text based work that sparks ideas and informs thinking. With a focus on shape/form, color/pattern, and image/text students will learn the process of design thinking, creative research, and visual communication through various methods of analog and digital problem solving. Presentations, assigned readings, and small group critiques will help students develop their own creative practice in design while gaining experience from concept development, sketching, digital layouts in Adobe Illustrator and Photoshop, editing, and printing. This course emphasizes personal creativity, experimentation, and developing an intentional studio practice. Long and short form assignments will provide foundational structure while students work to visualize their ideas, concepts, and aesthetic interests.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 239-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 272-01 (10042)Art of ChinaMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Kari Shepherdson-Scott1120

      Cross-listed with ASIA 272-01 (10043)

      > Details for ART 272-01

      This course examines the art and visual culture of China from the Neolithic era to the twenty-first century. Lectures and readings will teach methods of formal visual analysis as well as provide the opportunity for students to think critically about how scholars write the artistic history of the region. Through this class, students will engage with a broad array of media, from jade carvings, Buddhist cave painting, architecture, calligraphy and monumental landscape paintings to ceramics, imperial palaces, and contemporary installations. While examining the intended meanings and functions of these objects and spaces, we will discuss the varied contexts and value systems that have informed visual production in China.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ART 272-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 282-01 (10044)Art and Architecture of the Islamic WorldMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Serdar Yalçin1220

      Cross-listed with CLAS 282-01 (10045)

      > Details for ART 282-01

      This course will explore the art and architecture of the Islamic world from the time of the Prophet Mohammed to the 18th century. The lectures and class discussions will focus on culturally specific concepts of design and aesthetics in the vast geography from the Atlantic Ocean to the Indian Subcontinent, and analyze a variety of genres including architecture, glass, wood and metal working, painting, calligraphy and ceramics in their political and socio-economic contexts. Within these chronological, geographical and typological frameworks, this course will focus on special themes such as patronage, art and politics, figural representation, religious vs. secular art, and the impact of pre-Islamic Near Eastern heritage and Christianity on the development of Islamic forms and styles.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      ART 282-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 294-01 (10048)Soft Sculpture and the Fiber ArtsMeeting: M W 1:10 - 4:20 pmART 118Instructor: Lela Pierce510

      Seats saved for: 2 seniors, 2 juniors, 2 sophomores, 2 incoming First-Years

      > Details for ART 294-01

      This course serves as a hands-on introduction to some basic processes within the fiber arts and soft sculpture realm. Soft sculptures are three-dimensional forms made primarily from pliable materials such as fabric, paper, plastic, rubber, plant matter etc. as opposed to rigid materials such as wood, metal and stone. Soft sculptures can be draped, sewn and stuffed. Some techniques we may explore in this course include: fabric dyeing, hand and machine sewing techniques, basic weaving or unweaving techniques etc. Through hands-on experimentation, demonstrations, and discussion, students will examine how softness, texture, pattern, color and materiality can communicate concept, form, and emotion.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 294-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 294-02 (10049)Performance Art: History, Theory, and PraxisMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Joanna Inglot
      kt shorb      		1120

      Cross-listed with THDA 294-03 (10050)

      > Details for ART 294-02

      This interdisciplinary course reimagines the study and practice of performance art as a bridge between disciplines and a dialogue between theory and practice, mind and body, history and experimentation. Students will gain a rare opportunity to think, move, and create across traditional disciplinary boundaries within fine arts, investigating how artists transform the body into a site of creation, resistance, and social critique—from early avant-garde disruptions to contemporary, cross-cultural performances in galleries and in public spaces. Students will engage with foundational texts on embodiment, spectatorship, and the politics of visibility alongside hands-on explorations of performance strategies by globally significant artists, ensuring that they learn not only about historical figures such as Marina Abramović, Joseph Beuys, and Carolee Schneemann, but also with cutting-edge global artists and experimental collectives in performance art today.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism or U.S. Identities and Differences, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ART 294-02 course materials and textbooks
      ART 294-03 (10051)PhotoDesign: Image/TextMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmART 301Instructor: Mara Duvra210

      > Details for ART 294-03

      This interdisciplinary course explores the relationship between photography and design, focusing on the integration of visual imagery and typography to create work that is engaged and responsive to contemporary visual culture and shaped by current social, technological, and artistic contexts. This course introduces students to digital workflow, creative research, and technical skills as they learn to produce, manipulate, and combine photographic images with text across digital and print formats. Through presentations, studio projects, reading, and critiques students will learn how meaning is shaped through composition, sequencing, and the synthesis of image and language. Emphasis is placed on visual storytelling, experimental approaches, and design thinking. Students will gain experience with Adobe Illustrator, Photoshop, and Lightroom for image editing and design layout, while also engaging with contemporary and historical examples of Image/Text art and design practices in areas such as typography, editorial design, artist books, advertising, and social practice. *A digital SLR camera is recommended but not required for this course. Students can use their own camera or borrow one during the semester. Camera rentals are available at the start of classes.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 294-03 course materials and textbooks
      ART 294-04 (10885)Landscape Architectural Design Studio: Theory, Process, and ToolsMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amHUM 113Instructor: Tony Siebenaler-Ransom212

      Cross-listed with ENVI 294-05 (10884)

      > Details for ART 294-04

      This studio class will focus theory, process, tools, and most importantly will give you a chance to flex some design muscles. We will investigate theories of design that play a large role in the designing of our exterior spaces — everything outside of buildings: from parks to urban streetscapes. Adding to that, the history of design thinking that created many styles and places that we still hold in high esteem, coupled with the major artistic movements that still shape our aesthetic preferences. The studio will also focus on the tools employed by historic designers, but especially dig into how contemporary designers approach the process of transforming spaces into memorable places. Finally, throughout the course we will discuss the tools and methods of spatial design and most of the assignments will give you a hands-on opportunity to design spaces using the tools we have explored as you will explore develop your own process for design. Landscape design professionals come from all backgrounds, so this studio requires no specific background in art, design, or plants. All are welcome!

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 294-04 course materials and textbooks
      ART 294-05 (10976)WhistlesMeeting: M W 7:00 - 9:00 pmART 214Instructor: Ruthann Godollei88

      > Details for ART 294-05

      This course will explore the printmaking techniques of letterpress and screenprint as tools for social interaction and voicing one’s ideas in visual form. Inquiry into historic and contemporary examples of dynamic graphic art will include hands-on projects aimed at personal expression and the reverberations of the multiple which these print media can provide. No experience required. Contact the instructor directly for more information.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 294-05 course materials and textbooks
      ART 294-F1 (10046)Ancient Greek TempleMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmARTCOM 202Instructor: Serdar Yalçin-116

      First-Year Course only; cross-listed with CLAS 294-F1 (10047)

      > Details for ART 294-F1

      Classical forms of architecture have had a profound impact on the design of public and private buildings in our cities. Doric, Ionic, and Corinthian columns, triglyphs, metopes, and numerous other elements created in ancient Greece embellish our capitol buildings, opera houses, libraries, train stations and more. All these forms can be traced back to the ancient Greek temple. The association of this structure with sophistication and high culture is so deeply ingrained in the contemporary mind that the logo of the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) is based not on a mosque, a church, or another building form, but on the façade of a Greek temple, such as the famous Parthenon in Athens. This course will explore the formation of this distinctive building type and its functions in Greek society from the 8th century BCE to the Hellenistic period (ca. 330 - 30 BCE). What social, political, and economic processes led to the creation of the Greek temple forms? What role did interactions with cultures in the eastern Mediterranean—particularly Egypt and Western Asia—play in this process? Was there a standard form applied to all Greek temples, or did they vary? Who were their designers and patrons? And what historical and intellectual processes identified this iconic structure as the source of Western architectural tradition? Focusing on these key questions (and many others), this course will help students understand the importance of the majestic residences of Greek gods in shaping not only ancient urban and rural landscapes across the Mediterranean, but also modern ones, especially in Europe and North America.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ART 294-F1 course materials and textbooks
      ART 294-L1 (10887)Landscape Architectural Design Studio LabMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amHUM 113Instructor: Tony Siebenaler-Ransom212

      Cross-listed with ENVI 294-L1 (10886)

      > Details for ART 294-L1

      This studio class will focus theory, process, tools, and most importantly will give you a chance to flex some design muscles. We will investigate theories of design that play a large role in the designing of our exterior spaces — everything outside of buildings: from parks to urban streetscapes. Adding to that, the history of design thinking that created many styles and places that we still hold in high esteem, coupled with the major artistic movements that still shape our aesthetic preferences. The studio will also focus on the tools employed by historic designers, but especially dig into how contemporary designers approach the process of transforming spaces into memorable places. Finally, throughout the course we will discuss the tools and methods of spatial design and most of the assignments will give you a hands-on opportunity to design spaces using the tools we have explored as you will explore develop your own process for design. Landscape design professionals come from all backgrounds, so this studio requires no specific background in art, design, or plants. All are welcome!

      Course Materials:

      ART 294-L1 course materials and textbooks
      ART 327-01 (10052)Japanese Art and the (Inter)National ModernMeeting: M W F 3:30 - 4:30 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Kari Shepherdson-Scott516

      Cross-listed with ASIA 327-01 (10053)

      > Details for ART 327-01

      This course addresses the art and visual culture of Japan from the late nineteenth century through the twentieth century, a period of Japanese history marked by dramatic cultural, political, and social change.  The concepts of ’modernism’ and ’modernity’ were crucial elements in this change, sparking revolutionary shifts in the conception of art, empire, and identities (both personal and national) in Japan that would persist into the 21st century. Our class will discuss at length the visual arts from the 1850s to 1945, a time that saw foundational cultural, political, and social transformation in Japan as the nation developed from fledgling global power into a ultranationalist military hegemon in Asia; we will also consider artistic practices in the postwar era to understand the "crisis of the modern" that developed in the decades after the war as artists struggled to find their individual and national voices. Drawing on a diverse array of artistic forms and visual media, including painting, prints, sculpture, architecture, fashion, anime (Japanese cartoons), film, and photography, we will explore how themes such as trauma, nationalism, imperialism, war, protest, hybridity, and performativity have intersected with shifting notions of modern artistic and national identities inside and outside Japan. Prerequisite(s): No prerequisites required; First Years are welcome.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ART 327-01 course materials and textbooks
      ART 371-01 (10055)Painting IIMeeting: M W 8:10 - 11:20 amART 202Instructor: Chris WillcoxClosed 07

      > Details for ART 371-01

      A continuation of ART 234, expanding both technical and critical/theoretical knowledge through projects which demand the development of each student’s individual interests. Required projects will encourage increasing self-direction and development of the students’ own artistic vision. Readings will supplement students’  critical and theoretical understanding of historical and contemporary art practices. Frequent group discussions and critiques, as well as museum/gallery field trips. Three two-hour periods per week. Offered every other semester. Prerequisite(s): ART 234 .

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      ART 371-01 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Asian Studies

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      ASIA 113-01 (10636)Introduction to BuddhismMeeting: M W F 3:30 - 4:30 pmMAIN 111Instructor: Erik Davis1016

      Cross-listed with RELI 111-01 (10635); seats saved for 4 sophomores and 4 First-Years

      > Details for ASIA 113-01

      Buddhism is increasingly well-known in the USA, but what is it, and how does Buddhism encourage people to organize and think about their lives? Organized on the basis of the Eightfold Noble Path, with a focus of ’morals, the Buddhist psychology of mind, and meditation,’  this course offers an introduction to the personalities, teachings, and institutions of Buddhism. Beginning in India at the time of the Buddha, this course focuses on Theravada Buddhism, asking students to think historically, philosophically, and anthropologically. Many Friday sessions will be dedicated to an exploration of the variety of Buddhist meditative techniques.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 113-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 114-01 (10016)Introduction to Asian American StudiesMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmHUM 112Instructor: Jake Nagasawa716

      Cross-listed with AMST 114-01 (10015)

      > Details for ASIA 114-01

      This course introduces the basic issues and concepts of Asian American Studies. We begin by looking at the birth of Asian American Studies as a field in response to student protests in the late 1960s. We then proceed to an overview of Asian American history beginning with U.S. imperialism in Asia and moving up to the present moment. For the remainder of the course, we’ll focus on particular topics such as: the model minority myth; anti-Asian violence; multiracial Asian American identity; and Asian American activism. The approach of this course is interdisciplinary; it draws on theoretical and methodological insights from academic disciplines such as history, ethnic studies, and sociology.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 114-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 140-01 (10393)Introduction to East Asian CivilizationMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmMAIN 010Instructor: Chun Chen1320

      Cross-listed with HIST 140-01 (10392)

      > Details for ASIA 140-01

      This course introduces the cultures and societies of China, Japan and Korea from the earliest times to the present day. Primarily an introductory course for beginners in East Asian civilization, this course considers a variety of significant themes in religious, political, economic, social and cultural developments in the region.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 140-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 194-01 (10722)Reading Plays: Asian and Asian American PlaywrightsMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amTHEATR 101Instructor: Randy Reyes114

      Cross-listed with THDA 112-01 (10720) and AMST 102-01 (10721)

      > Details for ASIA 194-01

      Reading Plays guides students in close readings of dramatic literature, in particular of plays that are typically left out of the traditional theatrical canon. In this edition, students will learn about the socio-historical context of each play, and in-class exercises will introduce them to the foundation of script analysis: they will examine the play’s given circumstances, dialogue, dramatic action, characters, and style. Students will read a new play every week; assignments include weekly in-class writing exercises and short critical papers. Some playwrights we may explore are: Ayad Akhtar, Jaclyn Backhaus, Christopher Chen, Mashuq Mushtaq Deen, Prince Gomolvilas, David Henry Hwang, Naomi Iizuka, Rajiv Joseph, Hansol Jung, Haruna Lee, Kimber Lee, Young Jean Lee, Qui Nguyen, Jiehae Park, Lloyd Suh, Kristina Wong, Lauren Yee, and Chay Yew.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism or U.S. Identities and Differences

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 194-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 194-F1 (10152)Script, Character, Code: Technologies of WritingMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmHUM 217Instructor: Rivi Handler-Spitz116

      First-Year Course only; cross-listed with CHIN 194-F1 (10151)

      > Details for ASIA 194-F1

      How do technologies—from early writing tools to artificial intelligence—shape the way we read, write, and think? This course traces three thousand years of innovation in China, from oracle bones and paper to printing, the Chinese typewriter, Unicode, and AI. By exploring China’s major contributions to writing, literature, and the humanities, students gain historical perspectives for critically evaluating today’s algorithmic technologies, their ethical implications, and their impact on learning and human creativity.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 194-F1 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 211-01 (10536)Asian Philosophies: Chinese PhilosophyMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmMAIN 011Instructor: Bixin Guo1120

      Cross-listed with PHIL 211-01 (10535)

      > Details for ASIA 211-01

      Did Confucius really say all those things? What does it mean to call something "zen"? The popularity of mindfulness and meditation made "Eastern Philosophy" fashionable, but what exactly does that entail? This class will be an introduction to classical Chinese philosophy, focusing on Confucianism and its rivals (Daoism, Mohism, Buddhism). Many schools of thought in East Asia offered competing views on how to live a good life; we will explore these views and chart how they responded to each other. We’ll also see how Chinese thoughts were received and developed by Korean and Japanese philosophers and assess ongoing influences of these philosophies in East Asia and beyond. Texts with English translations.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 211-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 244-01 (10342)The Political Economy of AsiaMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmHUM 102Instructor: I-Chun Catherine Chang1216

      Cross-listed with GEOG 244-01 (10341)

      > Details for ASIA 244-01

      Whether the twenty-first century will be dominated by the "rising Asia" has spurred recurring debates in policy and academic circles. But what is Asia? How can we understand this diverse region where more than half of the world’s population resides? In this course, we will first deconstruct the idea of Asia as a cartographic entity to excavate the layered social-cultural meaning and geographical diversity of the "Asias." We will also place the "Asias" in a global context to reveal how contemporary Asia anchors the changing world political economy and cultural imaginations outside the West. We will begin with important theoretical debates on (East) Asian development that prevailed in the 1980s and 1990s, including discussions about the colonial past, the path-dependency of development and uneven industrialization, regional disparities and mega-urbanization. We will then use these debates as the foundation to explore the contemporary globalizing Asia. What are the important connections between Asian countries, and with other parts of the world? What are the roles of the "Asias" in international governance and geo-politics? Can China replace the United States as the dominant geo-economic power? These are the questions we will explore in this course.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 244-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 254-01 (10438)Japanese Film and Animation: From the Salaryman to the ShojoMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmHUM 110Instructor: Arthur Mitchell120

      Cross-listed with JAPA 254-01 (10437)

      > Details for ASIA 254-01

      This course surveys the history of Japanese film from the "golden age" of Japanese cinema to the contemporary transnational genre of anime. While introducing methodologies of film analysis and interpretation, it develops knowledge of how major works of Japanese film and animation have expressed and critiqued issues of modern Japanese society. In doing this, we trace the development of two related archetypes: the middle-class salaryman and the adolescent girl (shojo). These figures - as well as their incarnations as cyberpunks and mecha-warriors, sex workers and teen rebels - help us explore Japanese film’s engagement with the strictures of middle-class society, the constrained status of women, fantasy and escapism, sexuality and desire. Weekly screenings and discussion will be supplemented by readings in film theory and cultural criticism. Directors include Ozu Yasujiro, Akira Kurosawa, Oshima Nagisa, Miyazaki Hayao, Anno Hideaki, and Hosoda Mamoru. No prior knowledge of Japanese required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 254-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 258-01 (10159)Gender and Sexuality in ChinaMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmHUM 112Instructor: Xin Yang520

      Cross-listed with CHIN 258-01 (10158) and WGSS 258-01 (10160)

      > Details for ASIA 258-01

      How are masculinity and femininity defined and transformed in modern and contemporary Chinese culture? How is the social construction of gender related to a larger social context? Through a rigorous analysis of the content and structure of modern and contemporary novels and films, this course examines the literary representation of gender and sexuality and its relation to tumultuous social transformations. Themes to be explored include: May Fourth enlightenment, anti-Japanese war, Socialist construction, the Cultural Revolution, and the liberalization of the post-Mao era. This course seeks to help students develop critical views of Chinese society and culture from a gendered perspective and gain familiarity with major authors, genres, and literary movements. This course assumes no prior knowledge of China or Chinese, and all reading materials are in English.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 258-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 272-01 (10043)Art of ChinaMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Kari Shepherdson-Scott1120

      Cross-listed with ART 272-01 (10042)

      > Details for ASIA 272-01

      This course examines the art and visual culture of China from the Neolithic era to the twenty-first century. Lectures and readings will teach methods of formal visual analysis as well as provide the opportunity for students to think critically about how scholars write the artistic history of the region. Through this class, students will engage with a broad array of media, from jade carvings, Buddhist cave painting, architecture, calligraphy and monumental landscape paintings to ceramics, imperial palaces, and contemporary installations. While examining the intended meanings and functions of these objects and spaces, we will discuss the varied contexts and value systems that have informed visual production in China.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 272-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 282-01 (10440)Language and Identity in Japanese, Asian American, and Other CommunitiesMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmHUM 110Instructor: Satoko Suzuki1120

      Cross-listed with JAPA 282-01 (10439) and LING 282-01 (10441)

      > Details for ASIA 282-01

      This course aims to investigate the complex relationship between language and identity in Japanese, Asian American, and other diasporic communities. Language plays a significant role in conveying one’s identity, as do other semiotic means such as clothing. By exploring scholarship that considers language as it relates to identity, students will discover how speakers/writers use their agency in expressing their identity and how identity emerges as a social and cultural phenomenon. They will learn about multiple interrelated factors that affect how speakers/writers view and use language such as race, ethnicity, nationality, and multilingualism. No knowledge of Asian languages is required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 282-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 294-01 (10443)Gender and Japanese LanguageMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 110Instructor: Satoko Suzuki1520

      Cross-listed with JAPA 294-01 (10442), LING 294-01 (10444), and WGSS 294-02 (10445)

      > Details for ASIA 294-01

      When it comes to gender and English, people often think about third-person pronouns (they, she, he, ze, etc.), what others call you. In contrast, Japanese language speakers choose from a variety of first-person pronouns, what you call yourself. Some follow societal norms while others subvert them. This course examines how individuals and media creators make such linguistic choices (not just pronouns, but other expressions as well), and how these choices are fluid and based on such diverse factors as age, region, trend, rhetoric, and ideology. A very basic knowledge of Japanese is required, but all readings will be in English. Prerequisite: JAPA102 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 294-01 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 294-02 (10894)Indigenous Peoples in the Asia-Pacific: Environment, Society, and PoliticsMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 112Instructor: Arjun Guneratne
      Tom Robertson      		2330

      Cross-listed with ANTH 294-01 (10011) and ENVI 294-04 (10012)

      > Details for ASIA 294-02

      Asia and the Pacific Islands host more of the world’s Indigenous people than any other continent -- roughly 260 million out of 370 million worldwide (70 percent). In addition, Indigenous people in Asia generally make up the continent’s “poorest of the poor,” facing systemic disadvantage including limited access to healthcare, education, land, capital, and labor, as well as weaker political representation. In this course, we examine the lived experience of indigenous people in the Asia-Pacific through three analytical frames: their relationship to the state and the global order, their relationship to territory and the environment, and challenges to cultural survival. Along the way, we discuss colonialism and settler colonialism, colonial and contemporary representations of indigenous people, the challenges to both cultural and political survival of indigenous people posed by state formation, the challenges of resource extraction and globalization, conservation as a form of colonialism, and the creation of an indigenous diaspora. The course includes guest speakers and field trips.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 294-02 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 294-F1 (10660)Russia Goes EastMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmHUM 212Instructor: Maria Fedorova1616

      First-Year Course only; cross-listed with RUSS 294-F1 (10658) and HIST 294-F1 (10659)

      > Details for ASIA 294-F1

      Russia’s relations with the West often overshadow its deep connections with its eastern neighbors. From the 17th century onward, Russia - and later the Soviet Union - developed strategic approaches to control Siberia and the so-called Far East and assert its power in the Pacific, where it confronted other regional powers, such as China, Japan, and Korea. Given the current shifts in the global political order, understanding the historical connections between Russia, China, Japan, and Korea is more important than ever. This course examines the complexities of these relationships, with a particular emphasis on foreign policy. Key course themes include imperial Russian expansion into the Pacific region; colonization of indigenous populations; modernization projects (the Trans-Siberian Railway as a geopolitical tool); the clash of imperial interests in the Far East; the Russo-Japanese War; the 1917 revolution and its effect on China, Korea, and Japan; the White Movement in the Far East; the Soviet Union and Mongolia; the Pacific front during WWII; the Korean War and later relations with South and North Korea; Sino-Soviet relations in the 1960-80s; and the fragile partnerships after the fall of the Soviet Union.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 294-F1 course materials and textbooks
      ASIA 327-01 (10053)Japanese Art and the (Inter)National ModernMeeting: M W F 3:30 - 4:30 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Kari Shepherdson-Scott516

      Cross-listed with ART 327-01 (10052)

      > Details for ASIA 327-01

      This course addresses the art and visual culture of Japan from the late nineteenth century through the twentieth century, a period of Japanese history marked by dramatic cultural, political, and social change.  The concepts of ’modernism’ and ’modernity’ were crucial elements in this change, sparking revolutionary shifts in the conception of art, empire, and identities (both personal and national) in Japan that would persist into the 21st century. Our class will discuss at length the visual arts from the 1850s to 1945, a time that saw foundational cultural, political, and social transformation in Japan as the nation developed from fledgling global power into a ultranationalist military hegemon in Asia; we will also consider artistic practices in the postwar era to understand the "crisis of the modern" that developed in the decades after the war as artists struggled to find their individual and national voices. Drawing on a diverse array of artistic forms and visual media, including painting, prints, sculpture, architecture, fashion, anime (Japanese cartoons), film, and photography, we will explore how themes such as trauma, nationalism, imperialism, war, protest, hybridity, and performativity have intersected with shifting notions of modern artistic and national identities inside and outside Japan. Prerequisite(s): No prerequisites required; First Years are welcome.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      ASIA 327-01 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Biology

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      BIOL 170-01 (10057)Ecology and the EnvironmentMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmOLRI 250Instructor: Sebastiano De BonaClosed 128

      Cross-listed with ENVI 170-01 (10058)

      > Details for BIOL 170-01

      This course dives into a range of topics to study how species, populations, communities, ecosystems, and biomes function in our changing climate. We will emphasize biological nutrient and energy cycling, population dynamics, animal and plant species interactions, disturbances and response to disturbances, and ecology in urban and agricultural landscapes. We will examine Ecology under four conceptual ’lenses’: Climate Change, Environmental Justice, Land Use, and Ecosystem Services. These lenses provide critical insight into how scientists, policy makers, land managers, and other stakeholders evaluate complex ecological and environmental systems. Labs will be field and data-based, and emphasize the development of hypotheses, novel data collection at Ordway Field Station, and statistical analysis. Three hours lecture and one three-hour laboratory each week.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q3, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 170-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 170-02 (10059)Ecology and the EnvironmentMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amTHEATR 205Instructor: Stotra Chakrabarti128

Three hours lecture and one three-hour laboratory each week.

      BIOL 180-01 (10071)Biodiversity and EvolutionMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmTHEATR 205Instructor: Sarah Boyer1832

      > Details for BIOL 180-01

      An introduction to the diversity and history of life. We begin with basic evolutionary patterns and processes that have shaped living things, and then move on to the major groups of organisms (their morphology, physiology, reproductive cycles) and their evolutionary origins and relationships. Using recent findings from such diverse fields as molecular phylogenetics, developmental biology, and paleontology, this course introduces students to the major branches on the tree of life. Three lecture hours and one three-hour lab each week.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q2, Writing WC

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 180-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 180-L1 (10072)Biodiversity and Evolution LabMeeting: T 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 273Instructor: Michael Anderson916

      BIOL 190-01 (10074)GeneticsMeeting: M W F 8:30 - 9:30 amTHEATR 205Instructor: Linda Jeanguenin1731

      > Details for BIOL 190-01

      An introduction to the principles of genetics, including patterns of inheritance, structure and function of genetic material, flow of genetic information, and control of gene expression. This course also introduces students to the methodologies used in genetics research, such as gene mapping, gene expression techniques, DNA sequencing, introductory genomics, and gene manipulation. Bioethical and sociocultural issues that emerge from the applications of genetic concepts and methodologies are discussed. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory each week.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q2

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 190-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 190-02 (10075)GeneticsMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amTHEATR 202Instructor: Ruth Isenberg732

Bioethical and sociocultural issues that emerge from the applications of genetic concepts and methodologies are discussed. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory each week.

      BIOL 194-01 (10771)Leaves to LandscapesMeeting: M W 1:10 - 4:20 pmART 302Instructor: Megan Vossler
      Mary Heskel      		Closed 012

      Cross-listed with ART 194-01 (10770); Seniors restricted to 5 seats

      > Details for BIOL 194-01

      This interdisciplinary course explores the intersection of biology and art, by combining the study of plant ecology with a range of studio art practices. Students will investigate how plants respond to the environment, while developing skills in observational drawing, botanical illustration, visual journaling, and uncovering the uses of plants for pigments and drawing material. In addition, we will learn about plant form, function, physiology, and ecology across a range of environments, explore the history of artmaking as a type of scientific inquiry, and spend plenty of time outdoors. Fieldwork sessions will allow students to observe seasonal changes in plants, and the diversity of plant adaptations, while laboratory and studio sessions will facilitate experimentation and creativity in translating these observations into visual form. No prior drawing or biology experience is required, but an openness to integrating science and art is essential.

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 194-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 200-01 (10079)Cell BiologyMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amTHEATR 202Instructor: Phillip Rivera1932

      > Details for BIOL 200-01

      Cells are the foundational organizing units of all life on our planet.  In this course, we study the ways in which these nanomachines use the shapes and interactions of molecules to store and convert information, energy, and mass, and to communicate with their environments.  The workings of single-celled bacteria to multicellular organisms, and the complex behaviors of multi-species ecosystems all depend on the working of these miniscule molecular gears packed inside cells. This course covers the structures and behavior of biological molecules, the metabolic reactions that power life, the properties of the fluid, complex membranes that organize biological molecules in space and time, the ways in which information is sent and received within and between cells, and molecular-scale understandings of health and disease processes.  Three lecture hours each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 190; and CHEM 112 or CHEM 115

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 200-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 200-02 (10080)Cell BiologyMeeting: M W F 8:30 - 9:30 amTHEATR 202Instructor: Michelle Tianyi TongClosed 132

      > Details for BIOL 200-02

      Cells are the foundational organizing units of all life on our planet.  In this course, we study the ways in which these nanomachines use the shapes and interactions of molecules to store and convert information, energy, and mass, and to communicate with their environments.  The workings of single-celled bacteria to multicellular organisms, and the complex behaviors of multi-species ecosystems all depend on the working of these miniscule molecular gears packed inside cells. This course covers the structures and behavior of biological molecules, the metabolic reactions that power life, the properties of the fluid, complex membranes that organize biological molecules in space and time, the ways in which information is sent and received within and between cells, and molecular-scale understandings of health and disease processes.  Three lecture hours each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 190; and CHEM 112 or CHEM 115

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1

      BIOL 275-01 (10081)Outdoor Environmental Education in Theory, Policy and PracticeMeeting: M W 1:10 - 2:10 pmOLRI 243Instructor: Jerald Dosch614

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with ENVI 275-01 (10082) and EDUC 275-01 (10783)

      > Details for BIOL 275-01

      This course provides an introduction to outdoor education as an opportunity to promote social justice and environmental sustainability in a globalized world.  Informed by relevant philosophical, psychological, cultural and political-economic frameworks, in addition to critical issues in public education policy and practice, we will explore interdisciplinary approaches to outdoor environmental education appropriate for students across the K-12 continuum.  We will utilize the Katharine Ordway Natural History Study Area (Ordway Field Station) as an outdoor classroom and will adapt curriculum from the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources (DNR) and other outdoor education organizations to assist elementary school teachers and students in fulfilling Minnesota K-12 Academic Standards. Early in the semester, all students will participate in a weekend retreat at the Ordway Field Station. Weekly lab sessions will include field days during which course members design and implement educational experiences for elementary school children at Ordway, small group work days for preparing field day lesson plans, trips to local outdoor environmental education sites within the Twin Cities, and other experiential learning opportunities.  Weekly seminar sessions incorporating readings, reflective writing, and individual and small group projects complement the experiential aspects of the course. As the semester progresses, each course member will develop a curricular unit aimed at teaching an important environmental issue to diverse adolescents attending urban public schools.  The curricular unit is a significant undertaking that provides students with the opportunity to synthesize all aspects of the course material in a creative, pragmatic and integrative manner.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 275-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 275-L1 (10083)Outdoor Environmental Education LabMeeting: F 1:10 - 4:30 pmOLRI 243Instructor: Jerald Dosch614

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with ENVI 275-L1 (10084) and EDUC 275-L1 (10784)

      > Details for BIOL 275-L1

      This course provides an introduction to outdoor education as an opportunity to promote social justice and environmental sustainability in a globalized world.  Informed by relevant philosophical, psychological, cultural and political-economic frameworks, in addition to critical issues in public education policy and practice, we will explore interdisciplinary approaches to outdoor environmental education appropriate for students across the K-12 continuum.  We will utilize the Katharine Ordway Natural History Study Area (Ordway Field Station) as an outdoor classroom and will adapt curriculum from the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources (DNR) and other outdoor education organizations to assist elementary school teachers and students in fulfilling Minnesota K-12 Academic Standards. Early in the semester, all students will participate in a weekend retreat at the Ordway Field Station. Weekly lab sessions will include field days during which course members design and implement educational experiences for elementary school children at Ordway, small group work days for preparing field day lesson plans, trips to local outdoor environmental education sites within the Twin Cities, and other experiential learning opportunities.  Weekly seminar sessions incorporating readings, reflective writing, and individual and small group projects complement the experiential aspects of the course. As the semester progresses, each course member will develop a curricular unit aimed at teaching an important environmental issue to diverse adolescents attending urban public schools.  The curricular unit is a significant undertaking that provides students with the opportunity to synthesize all aspects of the course material in a creative, pragmatic and integrative manner.

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 275-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 302-01 (10085)Invertebrate Animal DiversityMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 270Instructor: Sarah Boyer215

      > Details for BIOL 302-01

      An introduction to the science of invertebrate zoology. The vast majority of animals are invertebrates, including beautiful and charismatic organisms such as corals and butterflies, and also pests and parasites such as mosquitoes and tapeworms. Students will become familiar with all major and some minor phyla of marine, terrestrial, and freshwater animals. Through lectures, discussions, field trips, dissections, and laboratory observations of live organisms students will learn to identify invertebrates and understand their anatomy, life cycles, and evolutionary history. Students will complete independent projects involving field observation and either collection or photography of local insects. Three lecture hours and one three-hour lab each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 180.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 302-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 302-L1 (10086)Invertebrate Animal Diversity LabMeeting: R 1:20 - 4:30 pmOLRI 273Instructor: Sarah Boyer215

      > Details for BIOL 302-L1

      An introduction to the science of invertebrate zoology. The vast majority of animals are invertebrates, including beautiful and charismatic organisms such as corals and butterflies, and also pests and parasites such as mosquitoes and tapeworms. Students will become familiar with all major and some minor phyla of marine, terrestrial, and freshwater animals. Through lectures, discussions, field trips, dissections, and laboratory observations of live organisms students will learn to identify invertebrates and understand their anatomy, life cycles, and evolutionary history. Students will complete independent projects involving field observation and either collection or photography of local insects. Three lecture hours and one three-hour lab each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 180.

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 302-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 304-01 (10087)NeuroanatomyMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 270Instructor: Emily Twedell116

      Permission of instructor required

      > Details for BIOL 304-01

      The structure and function of the vertebrate nervous system, with a focus on the human nervous system, will be explored through analysis of human brain specimens and sections, and artistic photographic and computer graphic representations of nervous system structures at both the microscopic and systems levels. This course is designed to provide an understanding of both the peripheral and central nervous system with a focus on brain, brainstem, and spinal cord structure and function through observation and study of normal brain tissue and discussion of clinical cases. This course is intended for students with a strong interest in neuroscience. Three lecture hours and one three-hour lab each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 190 and BIOL 200

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q3, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 304-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 304-L1 (10088)Neuroanatomy LabMeeting: T 1:20 - 4:30 pmOLRI 275Instructor: Emily TwedellClosed 116

      > Details for BIOL 304-L1

      The structure and function of the vertebrate nervous system, with a focus on the human nervous system, will be explored through analysis of human brain specimens and sections, and artistic photographic and computer graphic representations of nervous system structures at both the microscopic and systems levels. This course is designed to provide an understanding of both the peripheral and central nervous system with a focus on brain, brainstem, and spinal cord structure and function through observation and study of normal brain tissue and discussion of clinical cases. This course is intended for students with a strong interest in neuroscience. Three lecture hours and one three-hour lab each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 190 and BIOL 200

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 304-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 312-01 (10089)MicrobiologyMeeting: M W F 8:30 - 9:30 amTHEATR 203Instructor: Robin Shields-CutlerClosed 016

      > Details for BIOL 312-01

      This course is an introduction to the diverse field of microbiology, including prokaryotes, archaea, viruses, and eukaryotic microorganisms. We will explore the ways microbes interact with their environments, in particular their diverse and complex relationships with humans. The course includes regular discussion of primary literature and an emphasis on written and oral communication. In the laboratory, students will practice fundamental microbiology techniques and data analysis, and lead hypotheses-driven independent investigations. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 170, BIOL 190 and BIOL 200; CHEM 211 recommended.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 312-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 312-L1 (10090)Microbiology LabMeeting: R 1:20 - 4:30 pmOLRI 289Instructor: Robin Shields-CutlerClosed 016

      > Details for BIOL 312-L1

      This course is an introduction to the diverse field of microbiology, including prokaryotes, archaea, viruses, and eukaryotic microorganisms. We will explore the ways microbes interact with their environments, in particular their diverse and complex relationships with humans. The course includes regular discussion of primary literature and an emphasis on written and oral communication. In the laboratory, students will practice fundamental microbiology techniques and data analysis, and lead hypotheses-driven independent investigations. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 170, BIOL 190 and BIOL 200; CHEM 211 recommended.

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 312-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 316-01 (10091)Cell/Molecular NeuroscienceMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 301Instructor: Michelle Tianyi Tong016

      Permission of instructor required

      > Details for BIOL 316-01

      A study of the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying the biological basis of behavior. While particular emphasis is placed on the molecular and cellular components of the nervous system, these components are the foundation for the analysis of various systems. Discussion topics may include the role of neurotransmitters, neuromodulators and receptors in learning and memory, multiple sclerosis, Alzheimer’s disease and drug addiction. The laboratory will be used to introduce major research techniques in neurobiology. These techniques will be used in independently designed research projects. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 190 and BIOL 200

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 316-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 316-L1 (10092)Cellular and Molecular Neuroscience LabMeeting: T 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 275Instructor: Michelle Tianyi TongClosed 016

      > Details for BIOL 316-L1

      A study of the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying the biological basis of behavior. While particular emphasis is placed on the molecular and cellular components of the nervous system, these components are the foundation for the analysis of various systems. Discussion topics may include the role of neurotransmitters, neuromodulators and receptors in learning and memory, multiple sclerosis, Alzheimer’s disease and drug addiction. The laboratory will be used to introduce major research techniques in neurobiology. These techniques will be used in independently designed research projects. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 190 and BIOL 200

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 316-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 351-01 (10136)Biochemistry IMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 100Instructor: Kathryn SplanClosed 048

      Cross-listed with CHEM 351-01 (10135)

      > Details for BIOL 351-01

      A study of biological processes at the molecular level with an emphasis on the chemistry of biological molecules, elements of physical biochemistry, the structure of proteins, the mechanisms and kinetics of enzyme catalyzed reactions, and selected topics in intermediary metabolism, including the metabolism of carbohydrates and lipids. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 200 and CHEM 212, or permission of the instructor. Students must earn a C- or higher in prerequisite courses.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 351-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 351-L1 (10138)Biochemistry I LabMeeting: T 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 289Instructor: Kathryn SplanClosed 016

      Cross-listed with CHEM 351-L1 (10137)*

      Cross-listed with CHEM 351-L2 (10139)*
