      BIOL 351-L2 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 351-L3 (10142)Biochemistry I LabMeeting: R 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 289Instructor: Robin Shields-CutlerClosed 016

      > Details for BIOL 351-L3

      Cross-listed with CHEM 351-L3 (10141)*

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 351-L3 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 369-01 (10093)Developmental BiologyMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 273Instructor: Mary Montgomery716

      > Details for BIOL 369-01

      This course aims to integrate organismal, cellular, genetic and molecular approaches to the study of animal development. We will analyze a diversity of mechanisms, ranging from ones that set up pattern formation in the unfertilized egg to those governing morphogenesis of organ systems. Evolution of developmental mechanisms will also be discussed. The lab component will incorporate both descriptive and experimental embryological techniques. Three hours of lecture and one three-hour laboratory each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 180, BIOL 190 and BIOL 200, or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 369-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 369-L1 (10094)Developmental Biology LabMeeting: R 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 273Instructor: Mary Montgomery716

      > Details for BIOL 369-L1

      This course aims to integrate organismal, cellular, genetic and molecular approaches to the study of animal development. We will analyze a diversity of mechanisms, ranging from ones that set up pattern formation in the unfertilized egg to those governing morphogenesis of organ systems. Evolution of developmental mechanisms will also be discussed. The lab component will incorporate both descriptive and experimental embryological techniques. Three hours of lecture and one three-hour laboratory each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 180, BIOL 190 and BIOL 200, or permission of instructor.

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 369-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 380-01 (10095)Animal Behavior: Fundamentals and ApplicationsMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amTHEATR 101Instructor: Stotra ChakrabartiClosed 115

      > Details for BIOL 380-01

      Why do animals behave the way they do? Why do lions have manes while leopards don’t? Why do elephants and bees live in groups but many other species do not? Why does your friendly neighborhood squirrel get so busy late in the fall and again in the spring? Why do certain wolves ’fish’ but others never learn the technique? In this course we will explore the fundamentals of animal behavior and use that foundation to understand how we can better manage and conserve biodiversity. Outdoor and analytical labs will allow us to quantify animal behavior, develop ethograms, and understand species’ behavioral repertoires based on observation and manipulation of wolves, deer, bears, mountain lions and other species. Three hours of lecture plus three hours of lab each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 170. Recommended: STAT 155

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 380-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 380-L1 (10096)Animal Behavior LabMeeting: T 1:20 - 4:30 pm Instructor: Stotra ChakrabartiClosed 115

      > Details for BIOL 380-L1

      Why do animals behave the way they do? Why do lions have manes while leopards don’t? Why do elephants and bees live in groups but many other species do not? Why does your friendly neighborhood squirrel get so busy late in the fall and again in the spring? Why do certain wolves ’fish’ but others never learn the technique? In this course we will explore the fundamentals of animal behavior and use that foundation to understand how we can better manage and conserve biodiversity. Outdoor and analytical labs will allow us to quantify animal behavior, develop ethograms, and understand species’ behavioral repertoires based on observation and manipulation of wolves, deer, bears, mountain lions and other species. Three hours of lecture plus three hours of lab each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 170. Recommended: STAT 155

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 380-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 394-01 (10755)Invasion EcologyMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 243Instructor: Sebastiano De Bona315

      Cross-listed with ENVI 394-01 (10754)

      > Details for BIOL 394-01

      The negative impact of biological invasions is recognized as one of the key drivers of biodiversity loss, but are invasive species always bad? We will study how biological invasions happen, what makes invasive species so successful, and how their ecology and evolution shape their interactions with the ecosystems in which they become established. During the course, we will debunk some common misconceptions about invasion ecology and consider how invasive species are portrayed in the media. We will also explore the interplay between invasions and other global change factors such as urbanization, globalization of trade, and climate change. In the lab portion of this class, we will study first-hand how competitive interactions play out and how invasive species alter many biological communities around us. Prerequisite(s): BIOL/ENVI 170.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 394-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 394-L1 (10757)Invasion Ecology LabMeeting: R 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 253Instructor: Sebastiano De Bona315

      Cross-listed with ENVI 394-L1 (10756)

      > Details for BIOL 394-L1

      The negative impact of biological invasions is recognized as one of the key drivers of biodiversity loss, but are invasive species always bad? We will study how biological invasions happen, what makes invasive species so successful, and how their ecology and evolution shape their interactions with the ecosystems in which they become established. During the course, we will debunk some common misconceptions about invasion ecology and consider how invasive species are portrayed in the media. We will also explore the interplay between invasions and other global change factors such as urbanization, globalization of trade, and climate change. In the lab portion of this class, we will study first-hand how competitive interactions play out and how invasive species alter many biological communities around us.

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 394-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 412-01 (10097)Seminar in Cancer BiologyMeeting: M W F 12:00 - 1:00 pmOLRI 270Instructor: Mary MontgomeryClosed 016

      > Details for BIOL 412-01

      This seminar course centers on our understanding of the molecular events underlying the development of various cancers, including cell cycle control, DNA damage, tumor microenvironment, as well as therapeutic approaches to treatment, and associated difficulties. Two major goals of the course include developing an ability to understand cancerous transformation and learning to critically evaluate literature in the field. We will discuss primary articles and secondary reviews in a roundtable format, and students will present written and oral critiques of the literature and develop a final individual project. Three lecture/discussion hours each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 200, and junior or senior standing; or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 412-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 480-01 (10098)Research in PaleobiologyMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmOLRI 175Instructor: Kristina Curry Rogers06

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with GEOL 394-02 (10099)

      > Details for BIOL 480-01

      This course offers an opportunity to conduct collaborative research with a faculty member on topics in paleobiology. Students will actively engage with the primary literature and will design and pursue an investigation independently. The lecture component of the course will center on discussions of literature related to projects, as well as peer-evaluation of oral and written work. The laboratory component focuses on lab research and data analysis. Three lecture/discussion hours and one three-hour laboratory each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 180, GEOL 165, BIOL 365, or GEOL 300; junior or senior standing. Permission of instructor required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 480-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 480-L1 (10100)Research in Paleobiology LabMeeting: M W F 3:30 - 4:30 pmOLRI 175Instructor: Kristina Curry RogersClosed 06

      Cross-listed with GEOL 394-L1 (10101)

      > Details for BIOL 480-L1

      This course offers an opportunity to conduct collaborative research with a faculty member on topics in paleobiology. Students will actively engage with the primary literature and will design and pursue an investigation independently. The lecture component of the course will center on discussions of literature related to projects, as well as peer-evaluation of oral and written work. The laboratory component focuses on lab research and data analysis. Three lecture/discussion hours and one three-hour laboratory each week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 180 and GEOL 165, or GEOL 300; junior or senior standing, and permission of instructor.

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 480-L1 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 494-01 (10102)PsychoneuroimmunologyMeeting: R 1:20 - 4:30 pm Instructor: Phillip Rivera416

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with PSYC 494-01 (10900)

      > Details for BIOL 494-01

      Varies by semester. Consult the department or class schedule for current listing.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 494-01 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 494-02 (10772)Gene Editing: Fundamentals and ApplicationsMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmOLRI 270Instructor: Linda Jeanguenin1016

      > Details for BIOL 494-02

      Molecular biology is a cornerstone of modern biological sciences, serving as the foundation for both fundamental research, such as understanding protein function, and also applied research, such as developing genetically modified crops and gene therapies for rare diseases. This course is intended to deepen the understanding of molecular biology tools and techniques, with a particular focus on plant immunity. Through critical reading and analysis of primary scientific literature, students will explore the function of a specific plant receptor involved in immune responses. In the lab component, students will gain hands-on experience using the Golden Gate cloning method to construct a CRISPR vector targeting the plant immune receptor in the model plant Nicotiana benthamiana to generate gene knockouts. The course includes three hours of lecture and one three-hour lab session per week. Prerequisites: BIOL 190, BIOL 200 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 494-02 course materials and textbooks
      BIOL 494-L1 (10773)Gene Editing: Fundamentals and Applications LabMeeting: T 1:20 - 4:30 pm Instructor: Linda Jeanguenin1016

      > Details for BIOL 494-L1

      Molecular biology is a cornerstone of modern biological sciences, serving as the foundation for both fundamental research, such as understanding protein function, and also applied research, such as developing genetically modified crops and gene therapies for rare diseases. This course is intended to deepen the understanding of molecular biology tools and techniques, with a particular focus on plant immunity. Through critical reading and analysis of primary scientific literature, students will explore the function of a specific plant receptor involved in immune responses. In the lab component, students will gain hands-on experience using the Golden Gate cloning method to construct a CRISPR vector targeting the plant immune receptor in the model plant Nicotiana benthamiana to generate gene knockouts. The course includes three hours of lecture and one three-hour lab session per week. Prerequisites: BIOL 190, BIOL 200 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      BIOL 494-L1 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Chemistry

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      CHEM 111-01 (10103)General Chemistry I: Structure and EquilibriumMeeting: M W F 8:30 - 9:30 amOLRI 350Instructor: Susan Green1930

      15 seats saved for incoming First-Years

      > Details for CHEM 111-01

      This course offers a rigorous, foundational treatment of atoms and molecules. We study the nature of chemical bonding and how bonding gives rise to the three-dimensional structure of matter. We explore how the macroscopic properties of substances can be interpreted in terms of atomic and molecular structure. We also learn mathematical and conceptual tools for quantifying chemical equilibrium, with an emphasis on the reactions of acids and bases. Laboratory work reinforces concepts in lecture, and also provides a review of fundamental topics, such as stoichiometry, gas laws, and solution-phase reactions, that are essential for future course work in chemistry. Three lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week. All sections of class will have regular graded homework assignments and tests. Lab fee of $12 required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1, Writing WA, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 111-01 course materials and textbooks

      15 seats saved for incoming First-Years

We also learn mathematical and conceptual tools for quantifying chemical equilibrium, with an emphasis on the reactions of acids and bases. Laboratory work reinforces concepts in lecture, and also provides a review of fundamental topics, such as stoichiometry, gas laws, and solution-phase reactions, that are essential for future course work in chemistry. Three lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week. All sections of class will have regular graded homework assignments and tests. Lab fee of $12 required.

      CHEM 111-L2 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 111-L3 (10111)General Chemistry I LabMeeting: T 8:00 - 11:10 am Instructor: Marc Rodwogin2736

      18 seats saved for incoming First-Years

      > Details for CHEM 111-L3

      This course offers a rigorous, foundational treatment of atoms and molecules. We study the nature of chemical bonding and how bonding gives rise to the three-dimensional structure of matter. We explore how the macroscopic properties of substances can be interpreted in terms of atomic and molecular structure. We also learn mathematical and conceptual tools for quantifying chemical equilibrium, with an emphasis on the reactions of acids and bases. Laboratory work reinforces concepts in lecture, and also provides a review of fundamental topics, such as stoichiometry, gas laws, and solution-phase reactions, that are essential for future course work in chemistry. Three lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week. All sections of class will have regular graded homework assignments and tests. Lab fee of $12 required.

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 111-L3 course materials and textbooks

Three lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week. All sections of class will have regular graded homework assignments and tests. Lab fee of $12 required.

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 111-L7 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 115-01 (10116)Accelerated General ChemistryMeeting: M W F 8:30 - 9:30 amOLRI 250Instructor: Keith Kuwata1616

      Open only to incoming First-Years

      > Details for CHEM 115-01

      This course combines topics from both CHEM 111 and CHEM 112 and is meant to be an accelerated one-semester version of General Chemistry. The course begins with a rigorous treatment of atomic and molecular structure, including various models of chemical bonding. These ideas are applied to a thorough discussion of chemical thermodynamics and kinetics. Conceptual and mathematical methods for quantifying chemical equilibrium are also explored, as well as theoretical and practical aspects of nuclear chemistry. Three lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week. Students cannot receive credit for both this course and for either CHEM 111 or CHEM 112 . Lab fee of $12 required. Prerequisite(s): AP CHEM score of 4 or 5; IB CHEM score of 5, 6, or 7; or satisfactory performance on an online placement examination. Some knowledge of calculus is expected. Open only to incoming first-year students.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 115-01 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 115-L1 (10117)Accelerated General Chemistry LabMeeting: R 1:20 - 4:30 pmOLRI 347Instructor: Keith Kuwata1616

      > Details for CHEM 115-L1

      This course combines topics from both CHEM 111 and CHEM 112 and is meant to be an accelerated one-semester version of General Chemistry. The course begins with a rigorous treatment of atomic and molecular structure, including various models of chemical bonding. These ideas are applied to a thorough discussion of chemical thermodynamics and kinetics. Conceptual and mathematical methods for quantifying chemical equilibrium are also explored, as well as theoretical and practical aspects of nuclear chemistry. Three lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week. Students cannot receive credit for both this course and for either CHEM 111 or CHEM 112 . Lab fee of $12 required. Prerequisite(s): AP CHEM score of 4 or 5; IB CHEM score of 5, 6, or 7; or satisfactory performance on an online placement examination. Some knowledge of calculus is expected. Open only to incoming first-year students.

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 115-L1 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 123-01 (10118)Food ChemistryMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 301Instructor: Susan Green220

      > Details for CHEM 123-01

      Food chemistry is the application of chemical concepts to food, cooking and food processing. This course focuses on food and cooking with lots of in-class and out-of-class experimentation on texture, appearance, flavor and aroma of food. Chemical concepts such as structure, equilibrium and kinetics will be discussed in relation to cooking. Topics covered will include an overview of the chemical structure of fats, proteins, carbohydrates as well as the structure of flavor and aroma molecules. The equilibrium of the chemical reactions involved in ripening and cooking will be explored. Of particular interest are acid-base reactions, pH and the reactions involved in leavening, browning and fermentation. A portion of the course will be student led and depend on the interests of the class.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 123-01 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 211-01 (10119)Organic Chemistry IMeeting: M W F 8:30 - 9:30 amOLRI 100Instructor: Ronald Brisbois432

      > Details for CHEM 211-01

      This course develops a broad understanding of the theoretical concepts of organic chemistry. Molecular orbital theory and valence bond resonance theory are invoked to describe and explain the ramification of covalent bonding in organic molecules, as well as to rationalize fundamental noncovalent associations. Thermodynamic considerations of 3-dimensional molecular shape are discussed. Contemporary instrumental techniques for the assignment of molecular structure are a key focus. Modern mechanistic theory of organic chemical reactions is developed in the context of acid-base, nucleophilic substitution, elimination, and electrophilic addition reactions. Three lectures, four hours laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): CHEM 112 with a grade of C- or better or CHEM 115 with a grade of C- or better.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 211-01 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 211-02 (10120)Organic Chemistry IMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 250Instructor: Jeffrey BuenaflorClosed 232

      CHEM 300-01 (10129)Chemistry SeminarMeeting: W 3:30 - 4:30 pmOLRI 350Instructor: Thomas Varberg2570

      1 credit

      > Details for CHEM 300-01

      Presentations on current topics in the molecular sciences by campus visitors as well as by Macalester students and faculty. Chemistry majors will make an oral presentation during their senior year. One hour per week. Prerequisite(s): CHEM 212.

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 300-01 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 311-01 (10130)Thermodynamics and KineticsMeeting: M W 8:00 - 9:30 amOLRI 301Instructor: Thomas Varberg124

      > Details for CHEM 311-01

      This course covers topics in macroscopic physical chemistry dealing with the bulk properties of matter and energy. These topics include the properties of gases, the First and Second Laws of thermodynamics and their application to physical and chemical systems, phase and chemical equilibria, rates of reactions and chemical kinetics, and x-ray diffractometry. Three lectures and three hours of laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): CHEM 112 or CHEM 115, PHYS 227, and MATH 137. Student must earn a grade of C- or higher in prerequisite courses.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1, Quantitative Thinking Q2

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 311-01 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 311-L1 (10131)Thermodynamics and Kinetics LabMeeting: T 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 378Instructor: Thomas VarbergClosed 09

      > Details for CHEM 311-L1

Three lectures and three hours of laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): CHEM 112 or CHEM 115, PHYS 227, and MATH 137. Student must earn a grade of C- or higher in prerequisite courses.

      CHEM 311-L3 (10133)Thermodynamics and Kinetics LabMeeting: R 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 378Instructor: Keith KuwataClosed 08

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 311-L3 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 320-01 (10134)Computational ChemistryMeeting: T 1:20 - 4:30 pmOLRI 370Instructor: Keith Kuwata712

      > Details for CHEM 320-01

      Computation plays a key role in chemical research today, with many articles in the literature using computer modeling to make predictions of chemical behavior and to interpret experimental results. Arguably the most powerful subfield of computational chemistry is quantum chemistry-the application of quantum mechanics to atoms and molecules. This course has the following goals: (1) introducing the basic concepts of quantum chemistry; (2) illustrating the power and limitations of different quantum chemical methods; (3) providing opportunities to apply quantum chemistry to a variety of systems. The emphasis throughout the course will be on the use of computers to make predictions, instead of the mathematics and physics underlying quantum mechanics.  The second half of the course focuses on research projects possibly leading to publication. Prerequisite(s): CHEM 212 (Organic Chemistry II) with a grade of C- or better, or permission of the instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q2

      Course Materials:

      CHEM 320-01 course materials and textbooks
      CHEM 351-01 (10135)Biochemistry IMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 100Instructor: Kathryn SplanClosed 048

      Cross-listed with BIOL 351-01 (10136)

      > Details for CHEM 351-01

      A study of biological processes at the molecular level with an emphasis on the chemistry of biological molecules, elements of physical biochemistry, the structure of proteins, the mechanisms and kinetics of enzyme catalyzed reactions, and selected topics in intermediary metabolism, including the metabolism of carbohydrates and lipids. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 200 and CHEM 212, or permission of instructor. Students must earn a C- or higher in prerequisite courses.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      CHEM 411-01 (10143)Advanced Inorganic ChemistryMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmTHEATR 203Instructor: Kelsey Boyle424

      > Details for CHEM 411-01

      This course examines how modern theories of chemical bonding are applied to an understanding of the chemistry of the elements of the periodic table. Students explore chemical structures, reactions and spectra on the basis of molecular symmetry and group theory. Topics covered include chemical periodicity, coordination compounds, and organometallic complexes.Three lectures and three hours of laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): CHEM 312 with a grade of C- or better, or permission of the instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1

      CHIN 101-01 (10146)First Year Chinese IMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amHUM 112Instructor: Rivi Handler-Spitz1215

      > Details for CHIN 101-01

      This class is designed for beginners. Students will study Mandarin pronunciation, master pinyin, learn how to hand-write roughly 200 Chinese characters, and conduct simple conversations about dates and times, birthdays, family, hobbies, etc.

      Course Materials:

      Course Materials:

      CHIN 101-L3 course materials and textbooks
      CHIN 194-F1 (10151)Script, Character, Code: Technologies of WritingMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmHUM 217Instructor: Rivi Handler-Spitz116

      First-Year Course only; cross-listed with ASIA 194-F1 (10152)

      > Details for CHIN 194-F1

      How do technologies—from early writing tools to artificial intelligence—shape the way we read, write, and think? This course traces three thousand years of innovation in China, from oracle bones and paper to printing, the Chinese typewriter, Unicode, and AI. By exploring China’s major contributions to writing, literature, and the humanities, students gain historical perspectives for critically evaluating today’s algorithmic technologies, their ethical implications, and their impact on learning and human creativity.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      CHIN 194-F1 course materials and textbooks
      CHIN 203-01 (10153)Second Year Chinese IMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amHUM 111Instructor: Xin Yang1215

      > Details for CHIN 203-01

      This course is offered in the Fall semester only. As a prerequisite for entering Chinese 203, students should be able to read, speak (in accurate tones), understand, and write by hand and all the characters in chapters 1-14 of Integrated Chinese, Volume 1 and Integrated Chinese, Volume 2. Prerequisite(s): CHIN 102

      Course Materials:

      CHIN 203-01 course materials and textbooks
      CHIN 203-02 (10154)Second Year Chinese IMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amHUM 111Instructor: Xin Yang915

      > Details for CHIN 203-02

      This course is offered in the Fall semester only. As a prerequisite for entering Chinese 203, students should be able to read, speak (in accurate tones), understand, and write by hand and all the characters in chapters 1-14 of Integrated Chinese, Volume 1 and Integrated Chinese, Volume 2. Prerequisite(s): CHIN 102

      CHIN 203-L3 (10157)Second Year Chinese I LabMeeting: R 1:20 - 2:20 pmHUM 113Instructor: Ruoxi Song712

      > Details for CHIN 203-L3

      CHIN 258-01 (10158)Gender and Sexuality in ChinaMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmHUM 112Instructor: Xin Yang520

      Cross-listed with ASIA 258-01 (10159) and WGSS 258-01 (10160)

      > Details for CHIN 258-01

      How are masculinity and femininity defined and transformed in modern and contemporary Chinese culture? How is the social construction of gender related to a larger social context? Through a rigorous analysis of the content and structure of modern and contemporary novels and films, this course examines the literary representation of gender and sexuality and its relation to tumultuous social transformations. Themes to be explored include: May Fourth enlightenment, anti-Japanese war, Socialist construction, the Cultural Revolution, and the liberalization of the post-Mao era. This course seeks to help students develop critical views of Chinese society and culture from a gendered perspective and gain familiarity with major authors, genres, and literary movements. This course assumes no prior knowledge of China or Chinese, and all reading materials are in English.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      CHIN 258-01 course materials and textbooks
      CHIN 305-01 (10161)Third Year Chinese IMeeting: M W F 12:00 - 1:00 pmHUM 112Instructor: Patricia Anderson915

      > Details for CHIN 305-01

      This course is offered Fall semester only. As a prerequisite for entering Chinese 305, students should be able to read, speak (with standard, accurate tones), understand, and write from memory the characters in chapters 8-10 of Integrated Chinese, Vol. 3 and chapters 11-15 of Integrated Chinese, Volume 4. This amounts to roughly 700-900 characters. This course is conducted entirely in Chinese. From the start, students should be able to tell a simple story in Chinese, summarize a passage, and begin to formulate and express opinions on a wide variety of topics, including travel, Chinese holidays, education, and the environment. Prerequisite(s): CHIN 204.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      CHIN 305-01 course materials and textbooks

      > Details for CHIN 407-01

      This course is offered in the Fall semester only. As a prerequisite for entering Chinese 407 students should be able to read, speak (with accurate tones), understand, and write by hand most of the characters in chapters 1-4 of Reading Into a New China, Volume 1 and should be able to discuss and write essays on intellectually and linguistically challenging topics such as social issues and current events. Prerequisite(s): CHIN 306.

      Classical Mediterranean and Middle East

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      CLAS 111-01 (10165)Elementary Latin IMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmMAIN 011Instructor: Brian Lush1920

      > Details for CLAS 111-01

      This two-term sequence introduces the grammar and vocabulary of Latin, the language of the ancient Romans. Students learn through reading adapted passages, by breaking down grammatical structures into recognizable patterns, and through tutorials and drills. We aim to cover all basic grammar by the end of the year. In the second semester, students begin to read easy Latin such as the Bible, Pliny, Cornelius Nepos and/or Caesar.

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 111-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 111-L1 (10166)Elementary Latin I LabMeeting: T 3:00 - 4:00 pmMAIN 111Instructor: Brian Lush1920

      > Details for CLAS 111-L1

      This two-term sequence introduces the grammar and vocabulary of Latin, the language of the ancient Romans. Students learn through reading adapted passages, by breaking down grammatical structures into recognizable patterns, and through tutorials and drills. We aim to cover all basic grammar by the end of the year. In the second semester, students begin to read easy Latin such as the Bible, Pliny, Cornelius Nepos and/or Caesar.

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 111-L1 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 113-01 (10167)Elementary Arabic IMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amMAIN 009Instructor: Haci Gündüz1620

      > Details for CLAS 113-01

      This course introduces students to the Arabic language through an integrated approach that reflects how Arabic is used in real-life contexts. Students develop foundational proficiency in listening, speaking, reading, and writing while working with both Modern Standard Arabic (MSA)—used in formal communication—and Levantine Arabic, a widely spoken dialect used in everyday interaction. Through a communicative, proficiency-based, and flipped-classroom model, students learn new material outside of class and actively practice it through pair and group work during class time. The course emphasizes meaningful communication, cultural engagement, and the development of practical language skills for real-world use. Students also explore aspects of Arab cultures, including social practices, daily life, and contemporary issues. No prior knowledge of Arabic is required.

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 113-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 113-L1 (10168)Elementary Arabic I LabMeeting: T 8:35 - 9:35 amTHEATR 201Instructor: Haci Gündüz610

Through a communicative, proficiency-based, and flipped-classroom model, students learn new material outside of class and actively practice it through pair and group work during class time. The course emphasizes meaningful communication, cultural engagement, and the development of practical language skills for real-world use. Students also explore aspects of Arab cultures, including social practices, daily life, and contemporary issues. No prior knowledge of Arabic is required.

      CLAS 117-01 (10170)Elementary Hebrew IMeeting: M W F 12:00 - 1:00 pmMAIN 009Instructor: Nanette Goldman1720

      > Details for CLAS 117-01

      An introduction to the language and literature of classical Hebrew. The study of grammar and vocabulary is supplemented with practice in oral recitation and aural comprehension. Basic biblical texts are analyzed and translated, including selections from the books of Genesis and Ruth.

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 117-01 course materials and textbooks

      CLAS 135-01 (10173)India and RomeMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmTHEATR 205Instructor: Andrew Overman
      James Laine      		1225

      Cross-listed with RELI 135-01 (10174); 5 seats saved for First-Years

      > Details for CLAS 135-01

      This course is taught jointly between the department of Religious Studies and the department of Classics, by a specialist in the Roman East and a specialist in classical India. We will start on either side of this world, with Alexander the Great and Ashoka, exploring the relationship between empire and religion from Rome to India in the world’s crossroads for the thousand years between Alexander and the rise of Islam.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 135-01 course materials and textbooks

      CLAS 231-01 (10176)Intermediate Latin I: ProseMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amMAIN 003Instructor: Beth Severy-Hoven1220

      > Details for CLAS 231-01

      A thorough review of Latin grammar followed by a study of a Roman prose author or authors. Students build reading skills while gaining an appreciation for the literary and cultural aspects of Latin prose. Particular texts to be studied may include Petronius’ Satyricon , the letters and/or speeches of Cicero, letters of Pliny, or Livy’s History of Rome. Prerequisite(s): CLAS 212 or its equivalent.

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 231-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 241-01 (10177)Intermediate Arabic IMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmMAIN 011Instructor: Haci Gündüz1120

      > Details for CLAS 241-01

      This course builds on foundational Arabic skills by expanding students’ proficiency in speaking, listening, reading, and writing through an integrated approach to formal and spoken Arabic. Students continue to develop control of Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) for reading and writing while strengthening their ability to communicate in Levantine Arabic in conversational contexts. Using a communicative, proficiency-based, and flipped-classroom model, the course emphasizes active language use, increased grammatical accuracy, and the ability to express more complex ideas. Students engage with a range of authentic and adapted materials, including short texts, dialogues, and cultural content related to contemporary Arab societies. By the end of the course, students are expected to reach an intermediate level of proficiency and to navigate appropriately between formal and informal registers of Arabic. Prerequisite: CLAS 214 or equivalent.

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 241-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 241-L1 (10178)Intermediate Arabic I LabMeeting: R 10:10 - 11:10 am Instructor: Haci Gündüz1120

      > Details for CLAS 241-L1

      This course builds on foundational Arabic skills by expanding students’ proficiency in speaking, listening, reading, and writing through an integrated approach to formal and spoken Arabic. Students continue to develop control of Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) for reading and writing while strengthening their ability to communicate in Levantine Arabic in conversational contexts. Using a communicative, proficiency-based, and flipped-classroom model, the course emphasizes active language use, increased grammatical accuracy, and the ability to express more complex ideas. Students engage with a range of authentic and adapted materials, including short texts, dialogues, and cultural content related to contemporary Arab societies. By the end of the course, students are expected to reach an intermediate level of proficiency and to navigate appropriately between formal and informal registers of Arabic. Prerequisite: CLAS 214 or equivalent.

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 241-L1 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 260-01 (10031)Introduction to Art History I: From Prehistory to the Medieval PeriodMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amARTCOM 102Instructor: Serdar Yalçin1525

      Cross-listed with ART 160-01 (10030)

      > Details for CLAS 260-01

      ​This course surveys the visual and material culture of Europe, the Middle East and the broader Mediterranean world from prehistory through the late Medieval period, including early Christian, Jewish and Islamic cultures. We consider the artistic and archaeological remains from the Near East, Egypt, the Aegean, Greece, Etruria, Rome, and western Europe from a contextual perspective in order to understand each culture’s ideologies, and the diverse social and artistic purposes for which art and architecture was created and used.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 260-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 261-01 (10179)Intermediate Greek: ProseMeeting: M W F 3:30 - 4:30 pmMAIN 011Instructor: Brian Lush1520

      > Details for CLAS 261-01

      This third course in the ancient Greek language sequence involves review of basic grammar and extensive reading in works of ancient prose. Students will build familiarity with forms and vocabulary while gaining confidence as readers and an appreciation for ancient literature. Authors may include Plato, Herodotus, Attic orators, or Lucian. Prerequisite(s): CLAS 235 or equivalent.

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 261-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 282-01 (10045)Art and Architecture of the Islamic WorldMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Serdar Yalçin1220

      Cross-listed with ART 282-01 (10044)

      > Details for CLAS 282-01

      This course will explore the art and architecture of the Islamic world from the time of the Prophet Mohammed to the 18th century. The lectures and class discussions will focus on culturally specific concepts of design and aesthetics in the vast geography from the Atlantic Ocean to the Indian Subcontinent, and analyze a variety of genres including architecture, glass, wood and metal working, painting, calligraphy and ceramics in their political and socio-economic contexts. Within these chronological, geographical and typological frameworks, this course will focus on special themes such as patronage, art and politics, figural representation, religious vs. secular art, and the impact of pre-Islamic Near Eastern heritage and Christianity on the development of Islamic forms and styles.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 282-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 294-01 (10180)Before Homosexuality: Same-Sex Desire and Expression in Pre-Modern Islamicate WorldMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmTHEATR 201Instructor: Haci Gündüz420

      Cross-listed with RELI 294-03 (10760), WGSS 294-04 (10775), and HIST 294-04 (10782)

      > Details for CLAS 294-01

      This course explores how same-sex desire, eroticism, and gender variance were understood in pre-modern Islamicate societies before the emergence of modern sexual identities such as “homosexual” and “heterosexual.” Drawing on literary texts, legal treatises, medical writings, visual culture, and modern scholarship, students examine how desire was conceptualized in relation to gender, age, power, ethics, and social order. Rather than asking whether “gay people existed” in the past, the course asks how desire itself was structured, interpreted, and regulated and how modern categories of sexuality reshape our reading of pre-modern cultures. The course places Islamicate societies in global conversation with Greek, Christian, and modern European thought, emphasizing historical difference, cultural specificity, and the politics of interpretation.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 294-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 294-F1 (10047)Ancient Greek TempleMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmARTCOM 202Instructor: Serdar Yalçin-116

      First-Year Course only; cross-listed with ART 294-F1 (10046)

      > Details for CLAS 294-F1

      Classical forms of architecture have had a profound impact on the design of public and private buildings in our cities. Doric, Ionic, and Corinthian columns, triglyphs, metopes, and numerous other elements created in ancient Greece embellish our capitol buildings, opera houses, libraries, train stations and more. All these forms can be traced back to the ancient Greek temple. The association of this structure with sophistication and high culture is so deeply ingrained in the contemporary mind that the logo of the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) is based not on a mosque, a church, or another building form, but on the façade of a Greek temple, such as the famous Parthenon in Athens. This course will explore the formation of this distinctive building type and its functions in Greek society from the 8th century BCE to the Hellenistic period (ca. 330 - 30 BCE). What social, political, and economic processes led to the creation of the Greek temple forms? What role did interactions with cultures in the eastern Mediterranean—particularly Egypt and Western Asia—play in this process? Was there a standard form applied to all Greek temples, or did they vary? Who were their designers and patrons? And what historical and intellectual processes identified this iconic structure as the source of Western architectural tradition? Focusing on these key questions (and many others), this course will help students understand the importance of the majestic residences of Greek gods in shaping not only ancient urban and rural landscapes across the Mediterranean, but also modern ones, especially in Europe and North America.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 294-F1 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 483-01 (10181)Advanced Reading in LatinMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmMAIN 003Instructor: Nanette Goldman1720

      > Details for CLAS 483-01

      Students who pursue Latin at the advanced level will study closely one or more works and explore relevant problems in literary or textual criticism, linguistic, social or cultural history, and/or reception. Authors may include Tacitus, Seneca, Apuleius, Juvenal, Martial or others. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite(s): CLAS 332or equivalent.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 483-01 course materials and textbooks
      CLAS 490-01 (10182)Senior SeminarMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amMAIN 003Instructor: Beth Severy-Hoven1520

      > Details for CLAS 490-01

      The Senior Seminar caps the experience of being a Classics major. Students in all three tracks (Archaeology, Civilization, and Languages) join together to explore the history of the discipline and its relationship to their own histories and future plans. Students learn about, draft and critique documents useful for their next steps, including resumes, personal statements and cover letters, along with discussing selected readings in a seminar setting. Each student also works with an advisor from the department to develop and execute a major piece of work in their chosen field, Majors who have completed the Senior Seminar have thus executed a substantive independent project and can articulate the place of their Classics major within their personal and professional development. Prerequisite(s): Must be a declared Classics major

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      CLAS 490-01 course materials and textbooks

      COMP 112-01 (10183)Introduction to Data ScienceMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amTHEATR 203Instructor: Amin Alhashim224

      Cross-listed with STAT 112-01 (10184); seats saved for: 4 seniors, 6 juniors, 8 sophomores, 6 First-Years

      > Details for COMP 112-01

      This course provides an introduction to the handling, analysis, and interpretation of the big datasets now routinely being collected in science, commerce, and government. Students achieve facility with a sophisticated, technical computing environment. The course aligns with techniques being used in several courses in the natural and social sciences, statistics, and mathematics. The course is intended to be accessible to all students, regardless of background.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q2

      Course Materials:

      COMP 112-01 course materials and textbooks

      COMP 123-01 (10191)Core Concepts in Computer ScienceMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 258Instructor: Elizabeth Ernst425

      > Details for COMP 123-01

      This course introduces the field of computer science, including central concepts such as the design and implementation of algorithms and programs, testing and analyzing programs, the representation of information within the computer, and the role of abstraction and metaphor in computer science. The exploration of these central ideas will draw examples from a range of application areas including multimedia processing, turtle graphics, and text processing. Course work will use the Python programming language.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 123-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 123-02 (10192)Core Concepts in Computer ScienceMeeting: M W F 12:00 - 1:00 pmOLRI 258Instructor: Elizabeth Ernst1425

      > Details for COMP 123-02

      This course introduces the field of computer science, including central concepts such as the design and implementation of algorithms and programs, testing and analyzing programs, the representation of information within the computer, and the role of abstraction and metaphor in computer science. The exploration of these central ideas will draw examples from a range of application areas including multimedia processing, turtle graphics, and text processing. Course work will use the Python programming language.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 123-02 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 127-01 (10193)Object-Oriented Programming and AbstractionMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 256Instructor: Joslenne Peña1416

      > Details for COMP 127-01

      What happens as software grows in complexity? How do we break a program into manageable pieces? How do we write readable, maintainable code? This course is an introduction to the building blocks of software design: abstraction, decomposition, and encapsulation. Using object-oriented programming in Java, we will create graphics, games, and simulations, and explore natural language processing. Topics may include: classes, objects, polymorphism, inheritance, testing, refactoring, events, closures, streams, immutability, parallel programming, and version control. The course culminates in a student-designed project. Prerequisite(s): COMP 123 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 127-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 127-02 (10194)Object-Oriented Programming and AbstractionMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 256Instructor: Joslenne Peña416

      > Details for COMP 127-02

      What happens as software grows in complexity? How do we break a program into manageable pieces? How do we write readable, maintainable code? This course is an introduction to the building blocks of software design: abstraction, decomposition, and encapsulation. Using object-oriented programming in Java, we will create graphics, games, and simulations, and explore natural language processing. Topics may include: classes, objects, polymorphism, inheritance, testing, refactoring, events, closures, streams, immutability, parallel programming, and version control. The course culminates in a student-designed project. Prerequisite(s): COMP 123 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      > Details for COMP 127-03

      What happens as software grows in complexity? How do we break a program into manageable pieces? How do we write readable, maintainable code? This course is an introduction to the building blocks of software design: abstraction, decomposition, and encapsulation. Using object-oriented programming in Java, we will create graphics, games, and simulations, and explore natural language processing. Topics may include: classes, objects, polymorphism, inheritance, testing, refactoring, events, closures, streams, immutability, parallel programming, and version control.       Course Materials:

      COMP 127-03 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 128-01 (10196)Data StructuresMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 245Instructor: Abby Marsh1320

      > Details for COMP 128-01

      This course familiarizes students with the fundamental data structures in computer science. Using the Java programming language, students will study existing data structure implementations, implement their own data structures, and develop data-intensive applications. The course covers stacks, queues, lists, trees, heaps, hash tables, graphs, and the common algorithms that use these data structures. Students will also receive an introduction to basic complexity analysis (Big-O), learn the time complexity of different data structure operations, and gain experience in calculating the time complexity of programs that use data structures. Prerequisite(s): COMP 127 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 128-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 128-02 (10197)Data StructuresMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 245Instructor: Abby Marsh1220

      > Details for COMP 128-02

      This course familiarizes students with the fundamental data structures in computer science. Using the Java programming language, students will study existing data structure implementations, implement their own data structures, and develop data-intensive applications. The course covers stacks, queues, lists, trees, heaps, hash tables, graphs, and the common algorithms that use these data structures. Students will also receive an introduction to basic complexity analysis (Big-O), learn the time complexity of different data structure operations, and gain experience in calculating the time complexity of programs that use data structures. Prerequisite(s): COMP 127 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 128-02 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 194-01 (10421)Thinking Internationally About (and With) AI and ChatGPTMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmCARN 105Instructor: David Moore020

      Cross-listed with INTL 294-01 (10420)

      > Details for COMP 194-01

      The sudden recent rise of LLM-based artificial intelligence (AI), notably but not only ChatGPT, is having deep sociocultural, political, economic, and educational effects. In this course we will think about, and with, AI in a global frame. After asking “what is AI?” and reviewing its history, we will explore AI topics including environment, labor, higher education, cognitive surrender, consciousness, personhood, recursivity, hallucination, geopolitics, warfare, alignment, existential risk, the post-Mac professions, and AI in film and literature. Rather than keep AI at a distance, we’ll work with it: carefully, skeptically, critically, together. But often we’ll avoid screens altogether.

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      COMP 194-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 194-02 (10897)Philosophy of AIMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmMAIN 011Instructor: Bixin Guo415

      Cross-listed with PHIL 394-01 (10541)

      > Details for COMP 194-02

      Artificial Intelligence is one of the most exciting and fastest-moving technological fields today. This course examines philosophical questions related to AI. We will explore big-picture questions such as whether AIs can be conscious, whether we can control them, whether there might be an intelligence explosion. What, if anything, distinguishes intelligence from computation, prediction, or pattern recognition? How should we think about AI agency, autonomy, and responsibility? We will also consider ethical and social issues involving large language models, transparency and explainability, the ethical status of robots, and the ethics of deploying AI in high-stakes domains. No programming experience required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      COMP 194-02 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 194-03 (10898)Radical SoftwareMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmHUM 401Instructor: Rory Solomon1216

      Cross-listed with MCST 294-02 (10890)

      > Details for COMP 194-03

      This hybrid theory/practice seminar will trace the history of radical, activist, and countercultural uses of software and digital technology. From the milieu surrounding the Whole Earth Catalog, to hacker collectives such as Anonymous, to recent art and activism, we will explore efforts that have attempted to deploy software and its networks as anti-authoritarian or counter-hegemonic techniques. We will examine concepts that undergird the commitments of such actions, such as liberalism, freedom, sovereignty, prefigurative politics, and direct action. We will consider industry discourse to ask whether and how we might distinguish between radical software and corporate marketing messages around innovation and “disruption.” Readings will include texts by authors such as McKenzie Wark, Fred Turner, Gabriella Coleman, Alex Galloway, and Ruha Benjamin. Coursework will include hands-on projects comprising creative and critical use and misuse of software, such as coding custom web browser extensions and USB keystroke injection attacks. No prior computer programming experience is necessary.

      Course Materials:

      COMP 194-03 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 194-F1 (10198)Creative CodingMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmOLRI 256Instructor: Paul Cantrell017

      First-Year Course only

      > Details for COMP 194-F1

      Varies by semester. Consult the department or class schedule for current listing.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 194-F1 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 194-F2 (10199)Making, Prototyping and Physical ComputingMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmLIBR 250Instructor: Lauren Milne017

      First-Year Course only

      > Details for COMP 194-F2

      Course Materials:

      COMP 194-F2 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 212-01 (10200)Intermediate Data ScienceMeeting: M W F 12:00 - 1:00 pmTHEATR 203Instructor: Amin AlhashimClosed 120

      Cross-listed with STAT 212-01 (10201)

      > Details for COMP 212-01

      This second course in the data science curriculum emphasizes advanced data wrangling and manipulation, interactive visualization, writing functions, working with data in databases, version control, and data ethics. Through open-ended and interdisciplinary projects, students practice the constant feedback loop of asking questions of the data, manipulating the data to help answer the question, and then returning to more questions. Prerequisite(s): STAT 112 and COMP 123 and STAT 155; STAT 253 recommended but not required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 212-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 221-01 (10202)Algorithm Design and AnalysisMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 254Instructor: Lian Duan1125

      > Details for COMP 221-01

      This course offers an in-depth introduction to the design and analysis of algorithms. Students will work with algorithms in pseudocode, and will learn formal and informal methods for analyzing algorithm efficiency and correctness. Topics may include recursion, divide and conquer, dynamic programming, greedy methods, branch and bound, randomized, probabilistic, and parallel algorithms. Application areas include string processing, graphs, geometric problems, and optimization. This course will introduce computability topics including regular expressions, grammars and parsing, automata, nondeterminism, and NP completeness. Prerequisite(s): COMP 128 (or COMP 124, if previously taken) and MATH 279, or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 221-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 221-02 (10203)Algorithm Design and AnalysisMeeting: M W F 8:30 - 9:30 amOLRI 254Instructor: Lian Duan2025

      > Details for COMP 221-02

      This course offers an in-depth introduction to the design and analysis of algorithms. Students will work with algorithms in pseudocode, and will learn formal and informal methods for analyzing algorithm efficiency and correctness. Topics may include recursion, divide and conquer, dynamic programming, greedy methods, branch and bound, randomized, probabilistic, and parallel algorithms. Application areas include string processing, graphs, geometric problems, and optimization. This course will introduce computability topics including regular expressions, grammars and parsing, automata, nondeterminism, and NP completeness. Prerequisite(s): COMP 128 (or COMP 124, if previously taken) and MATH 279, or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 221-02 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 225-01 (10204)Software Design and DevelopmentMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmOLRI 245Instructor: Bret Jackson416

      > Details for COMP 225-01

      This course is an introduction to the problem of building software with humans and for humans. Students work in teams to design and implement a semester-long user-facing software project of their own invention. There are no limitations on topic or technology; on the contrary, students are responsible for imagining possibilities, articulating goals, and researching and selecting suitable technologies. The format resembles a studio art class, with in-class discussion guided by sharing and critiquing classmates’ ongoing work. Topics include communication, division of labor, user-centered design, human-computer interaction, product management, project management, iterative development, engineering tradeoffs, separation of concerns, code readability and maintainability, refactoring, testing, and version control. Teams give a public demonstration of their working projects at the end of the semester. Prerequisite(s): COMP 127 (COMP 128 recommended), or COMP 124 if previously taken, or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 225-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 240-01 (10205)Computer SystemsMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmOLRI 245Instructor: Susan FoxClosed 122

      > Details for COMP 240-01

      This course is an introduction to how computer systems work, including how a computer represents data, how code is compiled into instructions for the CPU, and how memory is organized. Students will learn to use the C programming language and assembly language. Prerequisite(s): COMP 127 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 240-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 364-01 (10206)Human-Computer InteractionMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmLIBR 250Instructor: Lauren Milne220

      > Details for COMP 364-01

      From doors we can’t figure out how to open to websites we can’t figure out how to navigate, we’ve all encountered counter-intuitive designs. So how do we create design systems that inspire joy instead of frustration? And how do we ensure that we design for everyone, including people who may be very different from ourselves? This course will teach techniques to improve our design of user interfaces, by centering the human in the design process. It will provide an introduction to the field of human-computer interaction and the design and evaluation of user interfaces. Students will learn methods for designing and prototyping interactive systems and some of the principles of creating good design based on human cognition. The class will be a mix of lectures, in-class activities and design critiques. The central focus of the course is a group project, in which students will formulate a design problem, explore potential design opportunities and tradeoffs, and iteratively evaluate and improve upon a digital prototype of their design. Prerequisite(s): COMP 123 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 364-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 381-01 (10207)Programming LanguagesMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amOLRI 254Instructor: Paul Cantrell1425

      > Details for COMP 381-01

      Why do people create different programming languages? What characteristics do languages have in common? What design decisions differentiate them, and what tradeoffs motivate those decisions? How do languages affect the style of code we write, our development processes, and the ways we think about software? In this course, we will examine a wide variety of programming languages, many briefly and a few in depth. We will compare how they approach topics such as type systems, abstraction, composition, state and mutability, access control, flow control, function dispatch, closures, metaprogramming, concurrency, memory management, compilation, and runtime environment. Prerequisite(s): COMP 128  (COMP 240 and COMP 361 useful but not required)

      Course Materials:

      COMP 381-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 440-01 (10208)Collective IntelligenceMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmOLRI 241Instructor: Shilad Sen420

      > Details for COMP 440-01

      This course introduces the theory and practice of data science applied to online communities such as Wikipedia, Facebook, and Twitter. Students will read and discuss recent academic research papers that analyze behavior on these websites and use computational simulation, machine learning, and data-mining techniques to analyze massive behavioral datasets in areas such as recommender systems, natural language processing, and tagging systems. This course counts as the capstone. Prerequisite(s): COMP 221; or both COMP 127 and STAT 253

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q3

      Course Materials:

      COMP 440-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 456-01 (10209)Projects in Data ScienceMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 258Instructor: Bret Jackson620

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with STAT 456-01 (10210)

      > Details for COMP 456-01

      This third course in the data science curriculum is a capstone course that emphasizes team-based learning through open-ended data science projects. Working with a team throughout the course of the semester you will take on an interdisciplinary in-depth data science project and gain experience in developing and refining research questions, identifying and wrangling datasets, and clearly presenting results and conclusions. Mini-lectures by the instructor, guest speakers, and students will present advanced topics that supplement and support team-based learning. Counts as a capstone course for the Computer Science major and the Data Science major. Prerequisite(s): COMP 212 and STAT 253

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 456-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 484-01 (10211)Introduction to Artificial IntelligenceMeeting: M W F 3:30 - 4:30 pmOLRI 258Instructor: Susan FoxClosed 020

      3 seats saved for juniors

      > Details for COMP 484-01

      An introduction to the basic principles and techniques of artificial intelligence. Topics will include specific AI techniques, a range of application areas, and connections between AI and other areas of study (i.e., philosophy, psychology). Techniques may include heuristic search, automated reasoning, machine learning, deliberative planning and behavior-based agent control. Application areas include robotics, games, knowledge representation, and natural language processing. This course involves programming in Python; students should have a basic familiarity with Python or be prepared to learn Python during the course. This course counts as the capstone. Prerequisite(s): COMP 221; or (COMP 127 and STAT 253); or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      COMP 484-01 course materials and textbooks
      COMP 487-01 (10212)Computer Security and PrivacyMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmOLRI 258Instructor: Abby Marsh820

      > Details for COMP 487-01

      An introduction to computer security and privacy. Topics will include privacy, threat modeling, software security, web tracking, web security, usable privacy and security, authentication, anonymity, network security, social engineering, the relationship of the law to security and privacy, and ethics. This course will include hands-on experience with security exploits in a Linux environment and student-led discussions of research papers. Students will complete a capstone project in a security- or privacy-related topic of their choice. Prerequisite(s): COMP 240

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      COMP 487-01 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Economics

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      ECON 113-01 (10213)Financial AccountingMeeting: T R 8:00 - 9:30 amCARN 304Instructor: Bridgit Jordan225

      > Details for ECON 113-01

      Accounting is the language of business. One of the objectives of this course is to learn that "language." The emphasis will be on understanding financial statements both for profit and non-profit organizations. International accounting, ethics and investment decisions are also covered. This course is designed for students who desire an understanding of the elements of accounting as a component of a liberal arts education as well as for those who would like to study further in accounting or business. This course counts as a Group B elective.

      Course Materials:

      ECON 113-01 course materials and textbooks
      ECON 113-02 (10214)Financial AccountingMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amCARN 304Instructor: Bridgit JordanClosed 225

      > Details for ECON 113-02

      Accounting is the language of business. One of the objectives of this course is to learn that "language." The emphasis will be on understanding financial statements both for profit and non-profit organizations. International accounting, ethics and investment decisions are also covered. This course is designed for students who desire an understanding of the elements of accounting as a component of a liberal arts education as well as for those who would like to study further in accounting or business. This course counts as a Group B elective.

      Course Materials:

      ECON 113-02 course materials and textbooks
      ECON 119-01 (10216)Principles of EconomicsMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amCARN 304Instructor: Jessica Kiser2025

      > Details for ECON 119-01

      Economics is the study of how people make decisions and how these decisions apply to real-world problems. Economics can help us understand income inequality within and across countries, the quality of the environment, unemployment, poverty, crime, health care, financial crises, technological change, inflation and many more issues. This course introduces the basic tools that economists use to explore these topics and will cover fundamental economic concepts like scarcity, supply and demand, costs and benefits, trade-offs, and incentives. This course is a one semester overview of both microeconomics (the study of choices firms and individual consumers make) and macroeconomics (the study of the economy as a whole). This course counts as a Group E economics course and is the prerequisite for 200-level E courses in the Economics Department.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q3

      Course Materials:

      ECON 119-01 course materials and textbooks
      ECON 119-02 (10217)Principles of EconomicsMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amCARN 304Instructor: Jessica Kiser2125

      > Details for ECON 119-02

      Economics is the study of how people make decisions and how these decisions apply to real-world problems. Economics can help us understand income inequality within and across countries, the quality of the environment, unemployment, poverty, crime, health care, financial crises, technological change, inflation and many more issues. This course introduces the basic tools that economists use to explore these topics and will cover fundamental economic concepts like scarcity, supply and demand, costs and benefits, trade-offs, and incentives. This course is a one semester overview of both microeconomics (the study of choices firms and individual consumers make) and macroeconomics (the study of the economy as a whole). This course counts as a Group E economics course and is the prerequisite for 200-level E courses in the Economics Department.

      ECON 119-03 (10218)Principles of EconomicsMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amCARN 305Instructor: Ioannis Koutsonikolis2525

      Economics is the study of how people make decisions and how these decisions apply to real-world problems.       ECON 119-03 course materials and textbooks
      ECON 119-04 (10219)Principles of EconomicsMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmCARN 305Instructor: Sarah West2125

      Economics is the study of how people make decisions and how these decisions apply to real-world problems. Economics can help us understand income inequality within and across countries, the quality of the environment, unemployment, poverty, crime, health care, financial crises, technological change, inflation and many more issues.       ECON 119-05 (10220)Principles of EconomicsMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmCARN 305Instructor: 2225

      > Details for ECON 119-05

      Economics is the study of how people make decisions and how these decisions apply to real-world problems. Economics can help us understand income inequality within and across countries, the quality of the environment, unemployment, poverty, crime, health care, financial crises, technological change, inflation and many more issues. This course introduces the basic tools that economists use to explore these topics and will cover fundamental economic concepts like scarcity, supply and demand, costs and benefits, trade-offs, and incentives.
