      MATH 375-01 (10479)Graph TheoryMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmOLRI 254Instructor: Kristin HeysseClosed 020

      > Details for MATH 375-01

      Graphs are mathematical structures which represent the relationships between objects in a set. Graph Theory falls under the umbrella of discrete mathematics and borrows methods from several areas of study to explore properties like the overall strength and complexity of the graph. Topics in this course include connectivity, graph coloring, trees, graph algorithms, and network flows. This course also discusses how these topics relate to graphs found in applications, such as social networks and the internet. Prerequisite(s): MATH 279

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      MATH 375-01 course materials and textbooks
      MATH 377-01 (10480)Real AnalysisMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amTHEATR 213Instructor: Taryn FlockClosed 016

      > Details for MATH 377-01

      Basic theory for the real numbers and the notions of limit, continuity, differentiation, integration, convergence, uniform convergence, and infinite series. Additional topics may include metric and normed linear spaces, point set topology, analytic number theory, Fourier series. Prerequisite(s): MATH 137 and one of: MATH 236, MATH 237, or MATH 279.

      MATH 474-01 (10883)Partial Differential EquationsMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmOLRI 245Instructor: Will MitchellClosed 016

      > Details for MATH 474-01

      Partial differential equations are powerful tools for modeling and simulation, with wide use in science and engineering. This course introduces some famous and well-understood equations (e.g. for modeling heat flow, waves, advection, and gravitation) and covers strategies for solving them on paper and by machine; applications in one, two and three space dimensions are covered. Nonlinear equations are introduced more briefly. Capstone projects allow students to apply or extend their knowledge to a problem of their choice. Prerequisite(s): MATH 312

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      MATH 474-01 course materials and textbooks
      MATH 494-02 (10483)Discrete OptimizationMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amOLRI 245Instructor: Kristin Heysse816

      > Details for MATH 494-02

      Discrete optimization applies techniques from combinatorics and graph theory, along with algorithmic thinking, to find the best solution to problems with finite structures. This course introduces the integer program as a powerful tool to solve these problems before diving into more specialized methods for canonical problems, including maximizing flow in a network, finding shortest paths, and creating optimal schedules. Spanning both theory and applied, this course develops both proofs of correctness for these solutions and methods of implementation with code. Students will apply or extend their knowledge of the area with a capstone project on the problem of their choice. Prerequisite(s): Math 236, Comp 123 (or above), and one of the following: Math 375 or Math 379, or permission of the instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      MATH 494-02 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Media and Cultural Studies

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      MCST 110-01 (10484)Texts and Power: Foundations of Media and Cultural StudiesMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmOLRI 300Instructor: John Kim1016

      > Details for MCST 110-01

      This course introduces students to the intellectual roots and contemporary applications of cultural studies, including critical media studies, focusing on the theoretical bases for analyses of power and meaning in production, texts, and reception. It includes primary readings in anti-racist, feminist, modern, postmodern, and queer cultural and social theory, and compares them to traditional approaches to the humanities. Designed as preparation for intermediate and advanced work grounded in cultural studies, the course is writing intensive, with special emphasis on developing skills in critical thinking and scholarly argumentation and documentation. Completion of or enrollment in MCST 110 is the prerequisite for majoring in media and cultural studies.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      MCST 110-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 114-01 (10485)News Reporting and WritingMeeting: M 7:00 - 10:00 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Howard Sinker620

      6 seats saved for incoming First-Years

      > Details for MCST 114-01

      This class gives an introduction to the many media platforms that are vital to contemporary journalism, and provides a strong foundation in news writing and reporting. It is taught by a veteran editor and writer, who is a Macalester graduate and currently a digital editor at the Minneapolis Star Tribune ( startribune.com ). Campus and Twin Cities communities are used as students plan, develop, report and write stories individually and in groups. Cookies are usually served in class.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WC

      Course Materials:

      MCST 114-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 128-01 (10486)Film Analysis/Visual CultureMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amHUM 401Instructor: Brad Stiffler224

      > Details for MCST 128-01

      This course introduces the aesthetics of film as well as selected issues in contemporary film studies. Its aesthetic approach isolates the features that constitute film as a distinct art form: narrative or non-narrative structure, staging, cinematography, editing, and sound. Topics in contemporary film studies that might be considered include one or more of the following: cultural studies and film, industrial organization and globalization, representations of gender and race, and theories of authorship, horror, and spectatorship. Several papers, a test covering basic film terms, and a short video project emphasizing abstract form are required. Suitable for first year students.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      MCST 128-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 194-01 (10889)Technologies of Mediation: From Oral to DigitalMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 111Instructor: Rory Solomon1316

      > Details for MCST 194-01

      How is culture determined by the material conditions of its production and circulation? In what ways do communication technologies shape the expressive possibilities of the "content" they contain? How is what we say a product of how we choose to say it? These questions, often described as inquiry into relationships between form and content, are central to the field of media studies. This class will provide an introduction to this field through critical analysis of two thousand years of communication technology, examining media forms including: oral culture, the written and printed word, photography, cinema, wireless signals, broadcasting, and digital networks. We will examine how analysis of mediation intersects with questions of race, class, indigeneity, gender, sexual orientation, and colonialism; and we will study concepts such as technopolitics, the public sphere, cybernetics, semiotics, technological determinism, affect, and aura. Readings will include texts by key figures in the field such as Walter Benjamin, Marshall McLuhan, Stuart Hall, Raymond Williams, Laura Mulvey, Susan Leigh-Star, Wendy Chun, and Armond Towns. Students will dilate their understanding of this field beyond "the media," which often simply signifies media industries, to include the broader concept of mediation itself. In so doing, we will consider media theory itself as a medium, and will speculate about how form/content framings shape our understanding of society and culture.

      Course Materials:

      MCST 194-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 194-F1 (10489)Engaging CinemaMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 401Instructor: Brad Stiffler1616

      First-Year Course only

      > Details for MCST 194-F1

      This course presents cinema as an art form and a social phenomenon that engages us and that we engage with. Our guiding principle will be that watching a film involves us in active practices of engagement, and that these practices shape us as political and social subjects. While we usually perform our roles as spectators in automatic and habitual ways, this class will teach the basic tools of film analysis and theory to help us become more critically aware and alive to our practices and their potential effects. Some of our work will involve watching and analyzing classic (such as Alfred Hitchcock’s Rear Window and Agnès Varda’s Cléo from 5 to 7) and contemporary cinema (such as RaMell Ross’ Nickel Boys and Kleber Mendonça Filho’s Pictures of Ghosts). We will learn to engage with these films through critical academic writing, connecting their formal or technical features to their potential meanings. And, while this is not a production-oriented class, we will also engage with cinema by making short abstract videos and writing conceptually about the process. Finally, our class will pay particular attention to the spaces and contexts where we engage with film, from small non-profit repertory theaters to art museums to film festivals to our individual private screens. Part of our class will involve visiting local film exhibition spaces, archives, and film festival events. As a culmination of our studies, we will design and organize our own film screening on campus, using what we’ve learned to curate an experience and invite others to engage with cinema along with us

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      MCST 194-F1 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 232-01 (10490)Fundamentals of Video ProductionMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmHUM 412Instructor: Morgan AdamsonClosed 112

      > Details for MCST 232-01

      This course is designed as a basic introduction to digital video production. The objective of the class is to familiarize students of film theory and history with the language of cinema from the standpoint of production in order to deepen your appreciation and knowledge of the technical aspects of film/video and to develop your capacity to use video as a tool for research and communication. In this way, the course will be a combination of technical instruction, critical engagement, and creative exploration. We will analyze and employ a variety of filmmaking techniques as well as constructing narrative and non-narrative strategies for doing so. The focus of the course will be to familiarize you with some basic conventions of experimental, documentary, and narrative cinema. In each assignment, you will be encouraged to think about how formal decisions enhance and further narrative or thematic elements. We will thus pay very close attention to formal aspects of cinematic production: mise-en-scene. cinematography, editing, and sound design. In addition to this attention to form, success in the class will be dependent on a commitment to working through the technical aspects of video production (camera operation, lighting, editing software) in order to create short, original video pieces.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      MCST 232-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 258-01 (10882)The Middle East Through FilmMeeting: M 7:00 - 10:00 pmCARN 06AInstructor: Jenna Rice Rahaim020

      Cross-listed with ANTH 249-01 (10798) and INTL 258-01 (10881)

      > Details for MCST 258-01

      This course provides an introduction to Middle Eastern history, politics, and cultural life through the lens of film. Over the course of the semester, we will examine the development of both local and regional cinematic traditions; the role of film in the production of collective memory of conflict and war; techniques and visual rhetorics of ethnographic film; and the power relations inherent in the cinematic gaze. Readings will offer an introduction to methods of film analysis as well as to the political and artistic history of the modern Middle East.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      MCST 258-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 284-01 (10024)Radical Reelism: Indigeneity, Politics, and Visual CultureMeeting: M 7:00 - 10:00 pmHUM 214Instructor: Kirisitina Sailiata416

      Cross-listed with AMST 284-01 (10023)

      > Details for MCST 284-01

      Radical Reelism serves as an introduction to Critical Indigenous studies. In this course, we will trace the roots and routes of contemporary Indigenous filmmaking and new media in the U.S. and globally. What is the history of visual representation for Indigenous people? How do we reconcile the relationship between the reel and real? What is visual sovereignty? And what can Indigenous filmmakers and film theory teach us about radicalism and the world we live in? We will seek answers to these questions through a centering of the radical visions, stories and aesthetics of contemporary Indigenous filmmakers and visual artists around the world.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences

      Course Materials:

      MCST 284-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 294-01 (10789)Digital Cultural HeritageMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmLIBR 250Instructor: Aisling Quigley916

      > Details for MCST 294-01

      Cultural heritage sites, including libraries, archives and museums, have existed in some shape or form for a very long time. Computers, on the other hand, have only been a part of these institutions for about sixty years. Although technologies offer more efficient and cost-effective ways to store and disseminate information and promise greater accessibility to materials, that doesn’t necessarily mean that they successfully facilitate the missions of these cultural heritage sites or the needs of their visitors. Why is this the case? Do digital technologies truly have the potential to decentralize and democratize these spaces? What can they tell us about what we value, as a culture? In this interdisciplinary course, we will reflect on the impact of digital technologies on cultural heritage sites, and museums in particular, starting in the 1960s and continuing through the present. Interwoven throughout the course, we will discuss decolonization, digital repatriation, obsolescence, cultural heritage and AI, and collective remembering. The course culminates in the creation of an online exhibition.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      Course Materials:

      MCST 294-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 294-02 (10890)Radical SoftwareMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmHUM 401Instructor: Rory Solomon1216

      Cross-listed with COMP 194-03 (10898)

      > Details for MCST 294-02

      This hybrid theory/practice seminar will trace the history of radical, activist, and countercultural uses of software and digital technology. From the milieu surrounding the Whole Earth Catalog, to hacker collectives such as Anonymous, to recent art and activism, we will explore efforts that have attempted to deploy software and its networks as anti-authoritarian or counter-hegemonic techniques. We will examine concepts that undergird the commitments of such actions, such as liberalism, freedom, sovereignty, prefigurative politics, and direct action. We will consider industry discourse to ask whether and how we might distinguish between radical software and corporate marketing messages around innovation and “disruption.” Readings will include texts by authors such as McKenzie Wark, Fred Turner, Gabriella Coleman, Alex Galloway, and Ruha Benjamin. Coursework will include hands-on projects comprising creative and critical use and misuse of software, such as coding custom web browser extensions and USB keystroke injection attacks. No prior computer programming experience is necessary.

      Course Materials:

      MCST 294-02 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 330-01 (10423)Surveillance and PowerMeeting: M 7:00 - 10:00 pmCARN 404Instructor: Alix JohnsonClosed 016

      Cross-listed with INTL 330-01 (10422)

      > Details for MCST 330-01

      This course considers surveillance as a social formation, inseparable from the theory and exercise of power. On the one hand, it takes up the pressing questions surveillance raises, from the development of cutting-edge technologies to the complex work of international regulation. On the other hand, it situates surveillance historically as central to projects of imperial conquest, state formation, and colonial rule. Engaging with theoretical works, primary sources, empirical studies and artistic renderings, students will grapple with problems of surveillance and power across geographic and historical contexts.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism

      Course Materials:

      MCST 330-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 388-01 (10491)Critical and Creative Practices of Everyday LifeMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 314Instructor: Morgan Adamson516

      Cross-listed with WGSS 394-02 (10492)

      > Details for MCST 388-01

      This course explores how philosophers, creative writers, artists, and filmmakers have taken everyday life as a point of departure for critical reflection and creative practice. Grounded in cultural studies and feminist approaches to knowledge production, we will examine the everyday as a lens into a wide range of topics, including power, the body, race, gender, sexuality, capitalism, and colonialism. While surveying a wide range of approaches, the course will focus on the emerging genre of Autotheory, which blends critical theory with autobiography and memoir, leading to new insights and modes of resistance. In doing so, we will derive inspiration from contemporary writers, filmmakers, and artists who break with traditional approaches to academic writing and research by using the everyday as raw material. Students will have the opportunity to carry out a creative research project in the form of a written essay, film, or other multimedia form.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      MCST 388-01 course materials and textbooks
      MCST 488-01 (10493)Advanced Topics SeminarMeeting: M 1:10 - 4:30 pmHUM 412Instructor: John Kim712

      > Details for MCST 488-01

      In the capstone seminar, students working on an independent project in line with the theme of the seminar share their scholarship, integrating what they have learned in the major, emphasizing knowledge gained in their focus area, as well as presenting their work at a concluding mini-conference. The capstone experience involves close analysis of cultural artifacts that examine at a higher level issues first raised in the introductory course. The department plans to offer two seminars every year, at least one in media studies, enabling students to select the seminar most relevant to their intellectual development. In exceptional cases, students with sufficient preparation may take the seminar prior to their senior year. Students may take more than one MCST senior seminar as long as content varies. Recent seminar topics have included: Image/Text: Metaphor, Myth and Power; Advanced Film Analysis; Advanced Studies in War and Media; Postmodernism, Identity and the Media; Whiteness and the Media; Advanced Queer Media. Prerequisite(s): MCST 110 - Texts and Power: Foundations of Media and Cultural Studies or permission of instructor. MCST 128 - Film Analysis/Visual Culture recommended for film studies seminars. Non-majors are welcome if they have taken MCST 110 or a comparable course.

      Course Materials:

      MCST 488-01 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Music

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      MUSI 101-01 (10494)Basic MusicianshipMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amMUSIC 228Instructor: Inga Chinilina1725

      10 seats saved for First-Years

      > Details for MUSI 101-01

      In this course students will learn the fundamentals of Western tonal music notation, engage foundational music theoretical concepts, and develop aural skills related to converting sound into notation (through ear training activities) and notation into sound (through sight singing). Topics covered include: pitch notation, clefs, octave designations, keyboard layout, simple and compound meters, major scales and key signatures, minor scales and key signatures, intervals, triads, seventh chords, diatonic church modes, several different chord labeling systems, and introductions to harmonic function and melodic structure. This course assumes no previous experience with notation and does not count toward the music major or minor. This course fulfills the College’s fine-arts distribution requirement. Fall semester only. (4 Credits)

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 101-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 102-01 (10769)Music AppreciationMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amMUSIC 228Instructor: Inga Chinilina1825

      > Details for MUSI 102-01

      This course introduces students to a range of ways to listen to music, as well as providing opportunities to think, talk, and write about musical compositions. The majority of works we will engage with will come from the Western “Classical” repertory, from the Medieval period to the beginning of the twentieth century. To better understand what we are listening to and articulate our listening experiences, we will learn about the technical aspects of music, such as melody, harmony, pitch, rhythm, and form. By the end of the course, students will be able to describe the features and aurally recognize music from the major historical eras, including the Medieval, Renaissance, Baroque, Classical, and Romantic periods. This course assumes no previous experience with music and does not count toward the music major or minor. This course fulfills the College’s fine-arts distribution requirement. No prerequisites.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 102-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 111-01 (10495)World MusicMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amMUSIC 228Instructor: Chuen-Fung Wong1825

      > Details for MUSI 111-01

      This gateway course for the music major/minor introduces students to traditional, folk, and pop music from major traditions in Africa, Asia, the Americans, Europe, and the Middle East. We explore music as both an artistic expression and a social practice, and examine how music making connects to broader cultural and human experiences. Through short assignments, quizzes, and a creative project, students develop foundational skills in critical listening, musical analysis, and writing about the world’s music. There is no prerequisite. Frequency: once a year.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 111-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 113-01 (10496)MusicianshipMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amMUSIC 219Instructor: Victoria Malawey2325

      10 seats saved for First-Years

      > Details for MUSI 113-01

      In this gateway course for the music major/minor, students will learn extensively about chord labeling systems and how diatonic harmonic progressions work in a variety of tonal genres, how to analyze and write two-part counterpoint in a tonal context, how to read and create full scores, and how to identify the most commonly encountered chromatic elements in tonal music, including secondary dominants and modulation to closely related keys. Students will apply knowledge in written and online exercises, analysis activities, and music composition. In addition, students will learn how to convert sound into notation and notation into sound through ear training activities, including aural exercises, dictation, and sight singing. Prerequisite: MUSI 101 or placement by exam. Frequency: Every semester

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 113-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 153-01 (10497)Electronic MusicMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmMUSIC 219Instructor: Reid KrugerClosed 012

      > Details for MUSI 153-01

      Electronic music composition explores the art of creating experimental sound compositions using analog and digital technology. Although we will survey the historical development of electronic music, the emphasis of the class is on composition, including multi-media and experimental work. The class format includes listening, discussion, lab sessions and a final concert showcasing works created throughout the semester.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 153-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 194-F1 (10498)Writing about ArtMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmMUSIC 228Instructor: Mark Mazullo1616

      First-Year Course only

      > Details for MUSI 194-F1

      In this writing workshop, we will practice transmuting artistic experience into words. We will look at visual art, listen to music, and watch theatrical and dance performances, and in response, we’ll practice different modes of incorporating our reactions into creative and critical writing. We’ll read fiction, poetry, personal essays, criticism, reviews, memoirs, etc. and we’ll try our hands at each form, gaining a sense of what is involved in describing the color of a cello’s sound, the gestures of a dancing body, or the intangible emotions we feel when we stand in front of a painting. In a final project, students will choose an artistic form and a mode of response and develop a larger-scale piece of writing.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WC

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 194-F1 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 220-F1 (10459)Language and MusicMeeting: M W F 8:30 - 9:30 amHUM 400Instructor: Morgan Sleeper1616

      First-Year Course only; cross-listed with LING 220-F1 (10458)

      > Details for MUSI 220-F1

      Language and music are two uniquely human enterprises with a number of parallels: both rely on sound and convention, display hierarchical organization and culturally-specific practices, and can convey both communicative and social meaning. This course examines the intersection of language and music from a linguistic perspective. We will engage with questions such as: How can syntax, phonology, and morphology change between spoken and sung language? How do speakers of tone languages understand lyrics in sung melodies? Is hip hop different in different languages? What does it mean to study melody and rhythm in speech? Can music help people learn languages? How do drum- and whistle-languages work? How does music contribute to language revitalization? No musical ability is required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WC

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 220-F1 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 233-01 (10499)Form and AnalysisMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amMUSIC 219Instructor: Victoria Malawey1925

      > Details for MUSI 233-01

      In this course, students will develop a basic understanding of and ability to analyze musical form and compositional processes, including fugue, binary, ternary, song forms, modified strophic, variations, sonata, rondo, and concerto. The course will introduce students to different analytic methods and apply analytic techniques to a variety of different music genres, with a focus on European tonal music from 1650–1850 and North American and European popular music from 1960 to the present. Students will learn to communicate effectively about music’s structure, orally and in writing. Prerequisite: MUSI 214 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 233-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 315-01 (10500)A Song of Love and Death: Opera in HistoryMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmMUSIC 228Instructor: Mark Mazullo616

      > Details for MUSI 315-01

      This course provides a critical and historical introduction to opera from the early eighteenth century to the present. No background in music is required: the course is meant to serve students wanting a first experience with the art form as well as those who come with some experience and wish to deepen their engagement. If you’ve ever been curious about what all the fuss is about opera, this course is for you! We will occasionally look at musical scores, but students without skills in reading Western musical notation may succeed in the course by focusing on other aspects of opera (e.g. the text of the opera and its source material, performance and staging, critical reception history) in their written work. Along with the close study of individual operas on a variety of subjects--myth and legend, politics and religion, the bourgeois center and its margins--we will read critical commentary that contextualizes these artworks within the history of ideas. We will be interested above all in the ways in which opera tells us what it sounds like, and feels like, to be human.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 315-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 354-01 (10501)Gender and MusicMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmMUSIC 219Instructor: Victoria Malawey012

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with WGSS 354-01 (10502)

      > Details for MUSI 354-01

      In this course, students will explore how gender is constructed in a variety of American and European musical styles and contexts, with an emphasis on popular genres. Learning objectives include for students to: (1) better understand the intersectional ways in which gender relates to and is informed by other aspects of identity formation, including class, race, and sexuality, (2) investigate issues that have affected women’s participation in musical life, such as musical canons, gendered musical discourse, and gender stereotypes, (3) explore contributions of trans and non-binary musicians, as well as issues that affect their musical lives, (4) interrogate constructions of gender, masculinity, and femininity as they relate to music, and (5) to develop reading comprehension, critical thinking skills, and argumentative writing skills.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 354-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 70-01 (10503)Wind SymphonyMeeting: M 7:15 - 8:45 pmMUSIC 116Instructor: Aaron Isakson5050

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 70-01

      The Macalester Wind Ensemble performs music in a wide variety of styles, from as early as the 17th century to the classics of band literature, to music composed within the last few years. It is open without audition to wind and percussion students with at least high-school level performance skills.

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 70-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 72-01 (10504)African Music EnsembleMeeting: T R 6:45 - 8:15 pmMUSIC 116Instructor: Sowah Mensah3030

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 72-01

      Study of traditional African music through performance modes of drumming, singing, flutes, marimba, gyil, and dancing. Instrumental and vocal pieces will be selected from a variety of musical styles such as unaccompanied vocal chants, songs, music from life cycle events, royal music and music for social and other festive occasions. The repertoire includes music from Ghana, Guinea, Nigeria, Tanzania and Zimbabwe.

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 72-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 74-01 (10505)Macalester Concert ChoirMeeting: M WR 4:45 - 6:15 pmMUSIC 113Instructor: Michael McGaghie5050

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 74-01

      The Concert Choir is the advanced, select vocal ensemble of Macalester College, directed by Michael McGaghie. Members are held to an exceptional standard of musicianship and commitment. The choir draws its repertoire from music of the last six centuries and tours annually through the United States or abroad. The ensemble commissions and premieres at least one new work every year. Membership is open to all Macalester students by audition. Three 90-minute rehearsals per week.

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 74-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 76-01 (10506)Macalester ChoraleMeeting: T 4:45 - 6:15 pmMUSIC 113
      R 6:30 - 8:00 pmMUSIC 113      		Instructor: Michael McGaghie8080

      Additional required meeting time on Thursdays from 6:30-8:00pm in Music 113. Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 76-01

      The Chorale is the large choral ensemble of Macalester College, directed by Michael McGaghie. The choir’s repertoire includes a cappella and accompanied music from various Western and non-Western vocal traditions. The Chorale and Concert Choir combine annually to perform a major work with orchestra. Membership is open to all Macalester students by audition. Two 90-minute rehearsals per week.

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 76-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 78-01 (10507)Asian Music EnsembleMeeting: TBA Instructor: Chuen-Fung Wong2020

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 78-01

      The Macalester Asian Music Ensemble performs chamber genres and repertoires from across Asia. Students may choose to specialize in one or more of the music traditions represented by the department’s instrument collections and faculty expertise. These include, but are not limited to, Javanese gamelan, East and Southeast Asian chamber instrumental music, as well as Indian, Middle Eastern, and Central Asian traditions. We perform two major concerts (fall and spring) every academic year, and take part in a number of College and outside events and performances.

      MUSI 82-01 (10509)Jazz/Popular Music CombosMeeting: M 7:00 - 9:00 pmMUSIC 121Instructor: Peter Hennig3030

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 82-01

      Jazz and Popular Music Combos are open to all who wish to concentrate on repertoire, improvisation and original music. Students will focus on developing their composition and performance skills while exploring many different styles of contemporary music. The combos present two concerts each year.

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 82-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 84-01 (10510)Pipe BandMeeting: W 6:30 - 10:00 pmMUSIC 116Instructor: Michael Breidenbach3030

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 84-01

      Bagpipe and highland drum lessons are available free of charge to all students. Beginners and musicians of any experience level are encouraged to participate in lessons and/or ensemble.  We perform traditional and modern music at concert and competitions.

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 84-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 86-01 (10511)Chamber EnsemblesMeeting: TBA Instructor: Chi-Chi Bestmann3030

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 86-01

      The Chamber Music program offers an opportunity for students to explore and perform repertoire for small ensembles. All instrumentalists and vocalists are eligible to participate and entrance is by audition or by arrangement with Mr. Mandarano. Students who are accepted into Chamber Music are placed in various ensembles and over the course of the semester immerse themselves in learning a challenging and varied repertoire. Rehearsal times are arranged based on schedule availability and individual practice is required. Ensembles receive periodic coaching from department faculty or professional studio instructors and there is least one performance required per semester. Examples of chamber ensemble include: string quartet, piano trio, woodwind quintet, brass sextet, piano trio, flute and clarinet choirs.

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 86-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 88-01 (10512)Macalester Symphony OrchestraMeeting: T R 4:45 - 6:15 pmMUSIC 116Instructor: Chi-Chi Bestmann7070

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 88-01

      The Macalester Symphony Orchestra prepares and performs a wide range of repertoire for symphony orchestra from the time of Bach to the present day, including the music of living composers. Playing in the orchestra blends individual and group achievement to build toward several challenging, rewarding performances each semester. Social interaction among the members of the orchestra is facilitated by various events, including orchestra dinners and a brief tour. In the spring semester, there is a concerto competition concert, featuring student soloists. Auditions for orchestra are held at the start of the fall semester. Space permitting, a limited number of students may be able to join at the start of the spring semester.

      Course Materials:

      MUSI 88-01 course materials and textbooks
      MUSI 90-01 (10513)Mac Early Music EnsembleMeeting: W 5:15 - 6:45 pmMUSIC 121Instructor: Clea Galhano2020

      Contact the ensemble director for registration information. Check the Music Dept website to find out if auditions are required.

      > Details for MUSI 90-01

      Historical performance has changed the aesthetic and musical experiences of audiences all over the world. The new ways to interpret music before 1750 has changed the way the audiences listen to this kind of music. Macalester Early Music Ensemble approaches instrumental and vocal music from Medieval, Renaissance and Baroque period. No audition needed.

      Development of keyboard (piano) technique and performance/analytical skills complementary both to the music theory curriculum and the general study of music. Technical foundations and repertoire study. 1 credit awarded upon successful completion of the semester. May be repeated for credit.

      Neuroscience

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      NSCI 488-01 (10515)Neuroscience CapstoneMeeting: M 12:00 - 1:00 pmOLRI 370Instructor: Darcy Burgund620

      2 credits

      > Details for NSCI 488-01

      Taken during the senior year, this 2-credit course provides a forum for students to prepare for post-graduation opportunities and create the presentation that each will showcase at the Neuroscience Presentation Session held every year. Students’ presentations will have a central focus on neuroscience and be based on their research/internship experience, or coursework to be approved by the capstone course instructor. The research/internship experience must be completed before the capstone course is taken. Prerequisite(s): Declared Neuroscience major.

      Course Materials:

      NSCI 488-01 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Philosophy

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      PHIL 100-01 (10532)Introduction to Philosophy: Love and FriendshipMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmMAIN 009Instructor: Geoffrey Gorham520

      5 seats saved for First-Years

      > Details for PHIL 100-01

      The Philosopher Aristotle said "without friends no one would choose to live, though they had all other goods". (Nicomachean Ethics, 1055a 5-7). It is not clear exactly what Aristotle means by this, but the high value he gives to friendship and love is shared by many other ancient Greek and Chinese Philosophers, such as Plato, Epicurus, and Confucius. This class will begin with an overview of central themes in ancient and contemporary philosophy -- epistemology, metaphysics and ethics -- and then undertake a detailed investigation of friendship and love: Why are friendship and love valuable? How do we become friends, and when should we break off friendships? With whom can we be friends: family members? pets? on-line friends? AI? Is ’romantic’ love real or merely a social construction? Should we love only one other, or many, or everyone? Could it be good for us to have no friends, or should we have as many friends as possible? How should we think philosophically about polyamory and asexuality? What role, if any, does gender play in four conceptions of friendship and love? What, if anything, do we owe to our friends and lovers? We will consider texts by Plato, Aristotle, Epicurus, Sappho, Confucius, Montaigne, Kant, Emerson, de Beauvoir, Sartre, bell hooks, as well as several contemporary philosophical perspectives on friendship and love. We will also read works of literature, such as Shakespeare’s Sonnets, and love poems of Emily Dickinson, and view together several films, such as Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind and Her. Your grade will be based on three short papers, two ’take-home’ examinations, reading responses (’convos’), and attendance/participation.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 100-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 100-F1 (10531)Introduction to Philosophy: Love and FriendshipMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amMAIN 001Instructor: Geoffrey Gorham1616

      First-Year Course only

      > Details for PHIL 100-F1

      The Philosopher Aristotle said "without friends no one would choose to live, though they had all other goods". (Nicomachean Ethics, 1055a 5-7). It is not clear exactly what Aristotle means by this, but the high value he gives to friendship and love is shared by many other ancient Greek and Chinese Philosophers, such as Plato, Epicurus, and Confucius. This class will begin with an overview of central themes in ancient and contemporary philosophy -- epistemology, metaphysics and ethics -- and then undertake a detailed investigation of friendship and love: Why are friendship and love valuable? How do we become friends, and when should we break off friendships? With whom can we be friends: family members? pets? on-line friends? AI? Is ’romantic’ love real or merely a social construction? Should we love only one other, or many, or everyone? Could it be good for us to have no friends, or should we have as many friends as possible? How should we think philosophically about polyamory and asexuality? What role, if any, does gender play in four conceptions of friendship and love? What, if anything, do we owe to our friends and lovers? We will consider texts by Plato, Aristotle, Epicurus, Sappho, Confucius, Montaigne, Kant, Emerson, de Beauvoir, Sartre, bell hooks, as well as several contemporary philosophical perspectives on friendship and love. We will also read works of literature, such as Shakespeare’s Sonnets, and love poems of Emily Dickinson, and view together several films, such as Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind and Her. Your grade will be based on three short papers, two ’take-home’ examinations, reading responses (’convos’), and attendance/participation.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 100-F1 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 121-01 (10534)Introduction to EthicsMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmMAIN 009Instructor: Samuel Asarnow920

      10 seats saved for First-Years

      > Details for PHIL 121-01

      What matters in life? Is happiness the only thing that matters? If so, whose happiness should I care about? Just my own? My family’s? Or everyone else’s, too? Does suffering matter, too? What about the suffering of non-human animals? Is it okay for me to make animals suffer in order for me to enjoy the pleasure of eating their flesh? Or how about the suffering of people who are really far away from me-say, on another continent? Is it okay for me to spend money on cool stuff for myself when instead I could donate it to help people who are suffering very badly far away? If things in life other than happiness matter, too, what are they? People who oppose torture think that it’s wrong to hurt one person really badly even in order to prevent a large number of people from being hurt. Are they right? Is it always wrong to treat someone as merely a means to an end? Is it in general wrong to do things to people without their consent? Why? When do people deserve to be praised or blamed for their actions? What kind of person should I be? Should I try to be happy? Or should I try to be virtuous? Is virtue its own reward? Or are we all inevitably faced with a choice between being virtuous and being happy? If we are faced with that choice, which one should we pick? In Ethics, we will talk about these questions, and others.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 121-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 121-F1 (10533)Introduction to EthicsMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmMAIN 009Instructor: Samuel Asarnow1616

      First-Year Course only

      > Details for PHIL 121-F1

      What matters in life? Is happiness the only thing that matters? If so, whose happiness should I care about? Just my own? My family’s? Or everyone else’s, too? Does suffering matter, too? What about the suffering of non-human animals? Is it okay for me to make animals suffer in order for me to enjoy the pleasure of eating their flesh? Or how about the suffering of people who are really far away from me-say, on another continent? Is it okay for me to spend money on cool stuff for myself when instead I could donate it to help people who are suffering very badly far away? If things in life other than happiness matter, too, what are they? People who oppose torture think that it’s wrong to hurt one person really badly even in order to prevent a large number of people from being hurt. Are they right? Is it always wrong to treat someone as merely a means to an end? Is it in general wrong to do things to people without their consent? Why? When do people deserve to be praised or blamed for their actions? What kind of person should I be? Should I try to be happy? Or should I try to be virtuous? Is virtue its own reward? Or are we all inevitably faced with a choice between being virtuous and being happy? If we are faced with that choice, which one should we pick? In Ethics, we will talk about these questions, and others.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 121-F1 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 211-01 (10535)Asian Philosophies: Chinese PhilosophyMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmMAIN 011Instructor: Bixin Guo1120

      Cross-listed with ASIA 211-01 (10536)

      > Details for PHIL 211-01

      Did Confucius really say all those things? What does it mean to call something "zen"? The popularity of mindfulness and meditation made "Eastern Philosophy" fashionable, but what exactly does that entail? This class will be an introduction to classical Chinese philosophy, focusing on Confucianism and its rivals (Daoism, Mohism, Buddhism). Many schools of thought in East Asia offered competing views on how to live a good life; we will explore these views and chart how they responded to each other. We’ll also see how Chinese thoughts were received and developed by Korean and Japanese philosophers and assess ongoing influences of these philosophies in East Asia and beyond. Texts with English translations.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 211-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 213-01 (10537)Philosophy of MindMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmHUM 215Instructor: Amanda Evans320

      > Details for PHIL 213-01

      What is the relationship between our conscious experience and the physical world? This question is one way of articulating the “mind-body problem”, which is arguably the central question of the philosophy of mind. This course provides a survey of the most influential historical and contemporary answers to the mind-body problem, including dualism, behaviorism, identity theory, and functionalism. Toward the end of the term, we will likely also cover topics such as panpsychism, the Simulation Argument, the puzzle of personal identity, and the question of whether a machine could ever be conscious. Students will leave the course with a firm foundation in the philosophy of mind and with a sharper perspective on what it means to be a thinking being.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 213-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 216-01 (10390)Reading MarxMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 213Instructor: David MartynClosed 118

      Cross-listed with GERM 316-01 (10389)

      > Details for PHIL 216-01

      For Marx, "philosophy" is really not a thing, since the world of ideas has no independent existence. And nevertheless, all Marx ever did was to write things, to do theory. In this course, we will come to know Marx’s theory as something that cannot be separated from the texts he wrote, texts that continue to challenge us in questioning the way we make sense of the world. We will read selections from Marx’s early writings on religion and alienation through the theory of ideology and commodity fetishism in tandem with texts by 20th-century thinkers and activists who critiqued and further developed Marx’s thought (Gramsci, Luxemburg, Du Bois, Angela Davis, Adorno). Requirements: Reading responses, problem sets, one short and one long paper. Core course for the Critical Theory Concentration. Taught in English.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 216-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 221-01 (10879)Environmental EthicsMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amMAIN 001Instructor: Aila O'Loughlin920

      Cross-listed with ENVI 221-01 (10880)

      > Details for PHIL 221-01

      The field of environmental ethics sparks a rich line of philosophical inquiry largely focused on the moral status of the natural world and the non-human entities within it. What reasons do we have to give moral consideration to the environment? And what do we mean when we say we have a moral duty toward the environment? Do we have moral duties to individuals within a species, or to species themselves, or to ecosystems, or to...? This course will invite you to reflect on key philosophical works that engage these and related questions. This course relies heavily on scholarship in Indigenous Environmental Justice and depending on the semester, may also include the debate over the ethics of wilderness preservation; global climate change and resource sustainability; rights of water and other so-called ’natural objects’ such as trees, plants, and rocks.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 221-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 394-01 (10541)Philosophy of AIMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmMAIN 011Instructor: Bixin Guo415

      Cross-listed with COMP 194-02 (10897)

      > Details for PHIL 394-01

      Artificial Intelligence is one of the most exciting and fastest-moving technological fields today. This course examines philosophical questions related to AI. We will explore big-picture questions such as whether AIs can be conscious, whether we can control them, whether there might be an intelligence explosion. What, if anything, distinguishes intelligence from computation, prediction, or pattern recognition? How should we think about AI agency, autonomy, and responsibility? We will also consider ethical and social issues involving large language models, transparency and explainability, the ethical status of robots, and the ethics of deploying AI in high-stakes domains. No programming experience required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 394-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 394-02 (10542)Indigenous PhilosophyMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amMAIN 011Instructor: Aila O'Loughlin715

      > Details for PHIL 394-02

      There are over 5000 nations of Indigenous peoples globally, including over 574 Native American tribal nations in what we now call the United States. This discussion-based course engages the rich diversity of thought in the tradition of Native American and Indigenous philosophy from both a historical as well as contemporary perspective. We will read from Indigenous philosophers on topics such as land, relations, being, and sovereignty to pose questions such as: What does it mean to be alive? What is the best way to know?, and, How do we live a good life in agreement with our surroundings?

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 394-02 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 394-03 (10787)Philosophy of PsychiatryMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmHUM 215Instructor: Amanda EvansClosed 015

      > Details for PHIL 394-03

      In what sense are mental illnesses genuine illnesses, and how do they differ from other bodily ailments? Is a psychiatric diagnosis the discovery of a natural biological fact, or is it a social judgment cloaked in the language of science? This course provides an overview of the philosophical study of mental disorder from a variety of angles, including the scientific, the ontological, and the sociopolitical. Throughout the term we will engage with interdisciplinary work in order to evaluate the scientific and normative status of mental disorder classifications. We will also examine the power dynamics between the institutions that employ these labels and the individuals affected by them. Students will develop the analytical tools necessary to navigate the complex intersection of science and values that is modern psychiatry. While the course will deal with sophisticated concepts, no specific philosophical background is required, and interested students from a variety of backgrounds are encouraged to enroll.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      PHIL 394-03 course materials and textbooks
      PHIL 489-01 (10543)Senior SeminarMeeting: M 7:00 - 10:00 pmMAIN 001Instructor: Geoffrey Gorham310

      > Details for PHIL 489-01

      A capstone experience in philosophy for senior majors. Each participant in the seminar will be expected to write an essay reflective of scholarly standards within the discipline on a question of their own choosing, within a collaborative and supportive environment. Participants in the seminar will help one another develop their capstone papers and prepare to present them publicly at the end of the semester. Readings may be assigned as appropriate with regard to student-identified topics. Prerequisite(s): Philosophy major and senior status, or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Physical Education

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      PE 03-01 (10516)Beginning Social DanceMeeting: M 7:00 - 8:30 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 1Instructor: Julie Kendle820

      > Details for PE 03-01

      Learn how to dance the Foxtrot, Waltz, Rumba and East Coast Swing! Learn to identify the music for each particular dance and dance to the music with a partner. Introduction to techniques unique to each dance. No partner is required, and all are welcome. No previous dance experience necessary. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 03-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 04-01 (10517)Karate IMeeting: M W 3:30 - 4:30 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 1Instructor: Gina Wood115

      > Details for PE 04-01

      This course is based on Japanese Shotokan Karate (JKA). Level 1 and level 2 work covers all basic stances, punches, strikes kicks and blocks required for ranking standards of the American Amateur Karate Federation. Students will also learn required partner drills (kumite) designed to build sparring skills as well as forms (kata) required for beginning to intermediate ranks-white through purple belt (8th kyu-5th kyu). Classes are taught in the same manner as at a karate dojo or studio so students are familiar with the etiquette and formality of that type of experience. Classes include stretching warm-ups and are vigorous and aerobic. Students should expect a demanding workout and should inform the teacher (Sensei) of any limitations they may have. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 04-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 06-01 (10518)Yoga IMeeting: M W 2:20 - 3:20 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 1Instructor: Sara AugustinClosed 120

      > Details for PE 06-01

      This class is based on Hatha Yoga. Class work is centered in physical posture (asana) practice and breath work (pranayama). These two limbs of yoga allow the student to become familiar with his/her own body and the way the minds inhabits it. Basic yoga poses (asana) are introduced and added to each week progressing from beginner poses to intermediate poses with advanced variations offered for more experienced students. Yoga class starts with centering breath work and moves into postures (asana) and movement (vinyasa) which warm up the muscles and open the joints as postures are explored. Props are employed to allow proper alignment. Class work ends with a cool down period to allow the student to come into balance and a relaxation (shavasana). Any level of fitness is appropriate! Inform instructor of limitations so practice can be adjusted for you. Come to the practice with an open mind and a desire to explore, embrace and inhabit your best self in a supportive, non-competitive space. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 06-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 06-02 (10519)Yoga IMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:00 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 1Instructor: Priscilla MomahClosed 120

      > Details for PE 06-02

      This class is based on Hatha Yoga. Class work is centered in physical posture (asana) practice and breath work (pranayama). These two limbs of yoga allow the student to become familiar with his/her own body and the way the minds inhabits it. Props are employed to allow proper alignment. Class work ends with a cool down period to allow the student to come into balance and a relaxation (shavasana). Any level of fitness is appropriate!       Course Materials:

      PE 06-02 course materials and textbooks
      PE 14-01 (10520)Karate IIMeeting: M W 3:30 - 4:30 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 1Instructor: Gina Wood710

      > Details for PE 14-01

      This course is based on Japanese Shotokan Karate (JKA). Level 1 and level 2 work covers all basic stances, punches, strikes kicks and blocks required for ranking standards of the American Amateur Karate Federation. Students will also learn required partner drills (kumite) designed to build sparring skills as well as forms (kata) required for beginning to intermediate ranks-white through purple belt (8th kyu-5th kyu). Classes are taught in the same manner as at a karate dojo or studio so students are familiar with the etiquette and formality of that type of experience. Classes include stretching warm-ups and are vigorous and aerobic. Students should expect a demanding workout and should inform the teacher (Sensei) of any limitations they may have. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 14-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 16-01 (10521)Yoga IIMeeting: T R 2:00 - 3:00 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 2Instructor: Sara Augustin1720

      > Details for PE 16-01

      This course integrates the core principles of Hatha Yoga-physical postures and breath work-to foster recovery, enhance performance, and promote overall physical well-being. Each session focuses on therapeutic sequences that release tension, improve mobility, and support balanced strength and flexibility. Classes begin with centering breath work to cultivate mindful awareness, followed by a series of guided postures and movements that gently warm up the body and target areas of common strain. Modifications and props are incorporated throughout to ensure safe, personalized alignment for all levels of experience. A structured cool-down phase allows time for the body to integrate the practice and restore equilibrium. Whether you’re looking to prevent injury, recover from physical exertion, or simply explore a mindful movement practice, join us in a supportive, non-competitive space where your individual needs and limitations are respected. Please inform the instructor of any physical concerns so your practice can be appropriately tailored. Anyone can take this class, regardless if you have taken Yoga I. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 16-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 18-01 (10522)PilatesMeeting: M W 4:45 - 5:45 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 1Instructor: Emma FitzsimmonsClosed 220

      > Details for PE 18-01

      Pilates is a mind-body discipline that uses non-impact exercises designed by Joseph Pilates to develop strength, flexibility, stability, coordination, and physical awareness. Practiced regularly, Pilates improves control and endurance in the entire body. Students learn Pilates breathing, principles, and its progressive repertoire, and how to apply them to correct their own movement patterns. Pilates enhances performance in other athletic activities and also in the activities of daily life. Students are expected to come ready to learn about a new way of moving with awareness. Regular attendance and focused attention are essential; cell phones are not allowed in the class. Dress for movement, but clothing should not be too baggy.  Classes are suitable for students of any level. No prior experience necessary. Pilates exercises can be modified to benefit all body types and backgrounds. All are welcome! Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 18-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 20-01 (10523)Weight TrainingMeeting: M W 2:20 - 3:20 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 2Instructor: Nicholas Brundy1425

      > Details for PE 20-01

      This class is an introduction to weight training. You will gain knowledge on how to properly use all of the equipment in the Deno Fitness Center. You will learn how to design basic strength workouts to meet your fitness goals. We will discuss the benefits of the different types of exercises and all of the different factors that influence your workout. We will also spend time learning technique and form on free weight exercises. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 20-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 23-01 (10524)Ultimate Frisbee 101Meeting: T R 2:20 - 3:20 pmLEOCTR FITNESS RMInstructor: Russell Adams
      Dina Kountoupes
      Jenelle Zumbusch      		3030

      > Details for PE 23-01

      Whether you are curious about getting into ultimate frisbee or you already are an active player who wants to improve your skills, this course will provide the information that will help you reach your goal. Ultimate 101 will cover all aspects of the game, from the rules and basic drills to more advanced skills and drills for those that already know the sport.  There will be both a classroom portion and an on-field portion to this class. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 23-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 24-01 (10525)Racquet SportsMeeting: T R 8:20 - 9:20 am Instructor: Matthew ReedClosed 012

      > Details for PE 24-01

      This course will provide the opportunity to learn the basic rules and strategies for playing the games of racquetball, badminton and pickleball. Throughout the course students will be introduced to the mechanics of the different types of swings and learn a variety of defensive and offensive shots for the different sports. Students will gain an understanding of all the rules of serving for both singles and doubles and will practice several different types of serves. Students will have the opportunity to play singles, doubles, and cutthroat games throughout the semester and will participate in tournament play toward the end of the term. The indoor track will be used for warm-up for our class activities. Graded S/N. Attendance is vital to success of the course. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 24-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 26-01 (10526)Tai Chi ChuanMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:20 pm Instructor: Phyllis Calph1320

      > Details for PE 26-01

      In this class you will be introduced to the ancient Chinese art of Tai Chi Ch’uan. The forms, comprised of many different postures or movements, are performed slowly and in a relaxed manner. But do not be fooled by the slower pace because you will definitely get a workout. Expectations of the class: develop an appreciation of the art of Tai Chi; develop increased coordination of mind and body; be able to demonstrate the correct movements of Yang style tai chi ch’uan. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 26-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 33-01 (10527)Salsa DanceMeeting: W 7:00 - 8:30 pmLEOCTR FIELDHOUSEInstructor: Don DeBoer120

      > Details for PE 33-01

      This course will introduce students to the basic elements of salsa dancing especially as it is done in nightclubs throughout the world. The course will include basic elements in addition to stylistic differences that may other related Latin dances. The class promises to be a good aerobic workout and will also take place in a variety of formats including: solo, line, partner, and group. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 33-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 55-01 (10528)BasketballMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:20 pmLEOCTR STUDIO 1Instructor: AD Donovan
      Richard Glesmann      		1420

      > Details for PE 55-01

      This course will introduce students to the fundamentals of individual and team basketball.  Students will develop basketball skills and learn the basic strategies of the game. Through this course they will develop a better appreciation for the game at all levels and help them develop a commitment to physical activity and well-being. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 55-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 56-01 (10529)BowlingMeeting: R 1:20 - 2:50 pmLEOCTR GYMInstructor: Donnie BrooksClosed 012

      > Details for PE 56-01

      The social and recreational values of a sport like bowling must be experienced to be appreciated. Individual help is given as needed. Bowling does not develop physical fitness, but other skills are involved and can be developed in an atmosphere that encourages social interaction. Open to all levels of experience.  Students will learn how to keep score and be taught the different techniques for bowling.  Part of the class will be taught in a classroom setting.  But the majority of class time will be off campus at a local bowling alley. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 56-01 course materials and textbooks
      PE 70-01 (10530)Monday Night FootballMeeting: M 7:00 - 8:30 pm Instructor: Phil Nicolaides1720

      > Details for PE 70-01

      This course is for individuals interested in learning the fundamentals for playing and coaching football. Students will learn the skills and techniques that are needed for each position on the field.  Students will also learn defensive, offensive, and special team philosophies and schemes.  This course will mainly be spent in the classroom, with some time spent on the football field for demonstrations and observation of practice. Not repeatable for credit.

      Course Materials:

      PE 70-01 course materials and textbooks

      Physics and Astronomy

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      PHYS 126-01 (10545)Introductory Physics IMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmOLRI 150Instructor: Saki KhanClosed 036

      > Details for PHYS 126-01

      Mechanics, heat and sound, including laboratory experiments and demonstrations. This course does not use calculus, has no prerequisites, and is appropriate for students pursuing pre-med or for students looking to satisfy a science distribution requirement.  This course does not count toward the physics major or minor.  This course is not appropriate for students who intend to major in physics, chemistry, or biology with the biochemistry emphasis.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1

This course does not use calculus, has no prerequisites, and is appropriate for students pursuing pre-med or for students looking to satisfy a science distribution requirement.  This course does not count toward the physics major or minor.  This course is not appropriate for students who intend to major in physics, chemistry, or biology with the biochemistry emphasis.

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 126-L1 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 126-L2 (10547)Introductory Physics I LabMeeting: R 1:20 - 4:30 pmOLRI 152Instructor: Brian AdamsClosed 018

      > Details for PHYS 126-L2

      Mechanics, heat and sound, including laboratory experiments and demonstrations. This course does not use calculus, has no prerequisites, and is appropriate for students pursuing pre-med or for students looking to satisfy a science distribution requirement.  This course does not count toward the physics major or minor.  This course is not appropriate for students who intend to major in physics, chemistry, or biology with the biochemistry emphasis.

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 126-L2 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 194-F1 (10791)Our Place in the CosmosMeeting: M W F 12:00 - 1:00 pmOLRI 150Instructor: Anna Williams017

      First-Year Course

      > Details for PHYS 194-F1

      Throughout the millennia of human thought we have sought to understand the origin of the universe, our existence, and the fate of it all. In this course we will grapple with these unknowns by determining our place in the cosmos. Our main focus will be a survey of our current understanding of physical cosmology: the evolution of the universe, from its beginning to its end. Along the way we will get to know our current location in the universe, St Paul, MN, and explore how the night sky shapes the lives of the people that call this land home. Basic gravitational physics and the interaction between light and matter will be introduced to provide the physical foundation for our exploration of cosmology. We will look at models of the universe, physical and some metaphysical, through astronomical observations of stars and galaxies and storytelling. This quantitative course will use mathematics at the introductory calculus level; high-school physics and calculus are recommended. Assignments will include problem sets, short papers, and a research paper.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 194-F1 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 226-01 (10548)Principles of Physics IMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmOLRI 150Instructor: Michelle Milne927

      > Details for PHYS 226-01

      A study of motion, including Newton’s Law of Motion, conservation of energy and momentum, rotational kinematics and dynamics, oscillations, waves in elastic media and thermal properties of matter. Three lectures and one two-hour laboratory per week. Students cannot receive credit for both this course and PHYS 221. Prerequisite(s): Concurrent or previous registration in MATH 135 or MATH 137; or a 4 or 5 on the Advanced Placement AB or BC Calculus exam.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1

      PHYS 226-L1 (10550)Principles of Physics I LabMeeting: T 9:10 - 11:10 amOLRI 152Instructor: Brian Adams718

      > Details for PHYS 226-L1

      A study of motion, including Newton’s Law of Motion, conservation of energy and momentum, rotational kinematics and dynamics, oscillations, waves in elastic media and thermal properties of matter. Three lectures and one two-hour laboratory per week. Students cannot receive credit for both this course and PHYS 221. Prerequisite(s): Concurrent or previous registration in MATH 135 or MATH 137; or a 4 or 5 on the Advanced Placement AB or BC Calculus exam.

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 226-L1 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 226-L2 (10551)Principles of Physics I LabMeeting: T 1:20 - 3:20 pmOLRI 152Instructor: Brian Adams318

      > Details for PHYS 226-L2

Three lectures and one two-hour laboratory per week. Students cannot receive credit for both this course and PHYS 221.       > Details for PHYS 226-L3

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 226-L3 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 331-01 (10553)Modern PhysicsMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 150Instructor: James Heyman824

      > Details for PHYS 331-01

      This course provides an introductory treatment of the exciting foundations of modern microscopic physics, including special relativity, quantum theory, atomic structure, nuclear structure and elementary particles. The primary goal of the course is to build the solid theoretical foundation in microscopic physics necessary for advanced studies in nearly all science disciplines. In addition to the theoretical treatment of the topics there will be laboratory exercises, which recreate the spirit and excitement of the pioneering experiments. Three lectures, one three-hour laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): PHYS 227.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q2

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 331-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 331-L1 (10554)Modern Physics LabMeeting: R 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 154Instructor: James Heyman512

      > Details for PHYS 331-L1

      This course provides an introductory treatment of the exciting foundations of modern microscopic physics, including special relativity, quantum theory, atomic structure, nuclear structure and elementary particles. The primary goal of the course is to build the solid theoretical foundation in microscopic physics necessary for advanced studies in nearly all science disciplines. In addition to the theoretical treatment of the topics there will be laboratory exercises, which recreate the spirit and excitement of the pioneering experiments. Three lectures, one three-hour laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): PHYS 227.

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 331-L1 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 331-L2 (10555)Modern Physics LabMeeting: R 1:20 - 4:30 pmOLRI 154Instructor: James Heyman312

      > Details for PHYS 331-L2

      This course provides an introductory treatment of the exciting foundations of modern microscopic physics, including special relativity, quantum theory, atomic structure, nuclear structure and elementary particles. The primary goal of the course is to build the solid theoretical foundation in microscopic physics necessary for advanced studies in nearly all science disciplines. In addition to the theoretical treatment of the topics there will be laboratory exercises, which recreate the spirit and excitement of the pioneering experiments. Three lectures, one three-hour laboratory per week. Prerequisite(s): PHYS 227.

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 331-L2 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 370-01 (10556)Computational PhysicsMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmOLRI 101Instructor: Tonnis ter Veldhuis1424

      > Details for PHYS 370-01

      This course introduces students to coding and computational methods, focusing on developing computation-based skills that are critical for practicing physicists. Students process experimental data using statistical tools, study the implications of realistic physical models using a toolbox of numerical methods, and visualize information in meaningful formats. The course starts with an introduction to coding (in Python) and a survey of data manipulation tools (e.g., reading, writing, analyzing statistically, etc.). The focus is on skills that help students in labs, research, and anywhere else they need to extract physical insight from data. After the introduction to data analysis, the course transitions into a survey of numerical techniques which facilitate quantitative analysis of theoretical models describing complex physical phenomena, thereby creating a bridge between experimental/observational and theoretical physics. The lab provides hands-on experience in the application of computational skills discussed in lecture to realistic physical models drawn from a wide range of physics subdisciplines. Projects in the lab are implemented using Jupyter notebooks, via the Google Colaboratory environment. Students write documentation in Latex and Markdown. No previous experience with any of these is assumed. Prerequisite(s): PHYS 227

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      PHYS 370-01 course materials and textbooks
      PHYS 370-L1 (10557)Computational Physics LabMeeting: R 8:00 - 11:10 amOLRI 101Instructor: Tonnis ter Veldhuis612

After the introduction to data analysis, the course transitions into a survey of numerical techniques which facilitate quantitative analysis of theoretical models describing complex physical phenomena, thereby creating a bridge between experimental/observational and theoretical physics.
