      STAT 212-01 (10201)Intermediate Data ScienceMeeting: M W F 12:00 - 1:00 pmTHEATR 203Instructor: Amin AlhashimClosed 120

      Cross-listed with COMP 212-01 (10200)

      > Details for STAT 212-01

      This second course in the data science curriculum emphasizes advanced data wrangling and manipulation, interactive visualization, writing functions, working with data in databases, version control, and data ethics. Through open-ended and interdisciplinary projects, students practice the constant feedback loop of asking questions of the data, manipulating the data to help answer the question, and then returning to more questions. Prerequisite(s): COMP 112 and COMP 123 and STAT 155; STAT 253 recommended but not required.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      STAT 212-01 course materials and textbooks
      STAT 253-01 (10712)Statistical Machine LearningMeeting: T R 8:00 - 9:30 amOLRI 254Instructor: Alicia JohnsonClosed 120

      > Details for STAT 253-01

      The linear and logistic modeling techniques from STAT 155 are augmented with the three foundational machine learning tasks: regression, classification, and clustering.  The course explores techniques central to these tasks, including methods of data exploration, supervised and unsupervised learning, parametric and nonparametric modeling, and model training and evaluation.  As required by the application of these sophisticated techniques, the course also introduces foundational statistical computer programming concepts. Prerequisite(s): STAT 155.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      STAT 253-01 course materials and textbooks
      STAT 253-02 (10713)Statistical Machine LearningMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amOLRI 254Instructor: Alicia JohnsonClosed 120

      > Details for STAT 253-02

      The linear and logistic modeling techniques from STAT 155 are augmented with the three foundational machine learning tasks: regression, classification, and clustering.  The course explores techniques central to these tasks, including methods of data exploration, supervised and unsupervised learning, parametric and nonparametric modeling, and model training and evaluation.  As required by the application of these sophisticated techniques, the course also introduces foundational statistical computer programming concepts. Prerequisite(s): STAT 155.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      STAT 253-02 course materials and textbooks
      STAT 354-01 (10714)ProbabilityMeeting: T R 8:00 - 9:30 amTHEATR 203Instructor: Alexander Hanhart120

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with MATH 354-01 (10715)

      > Details for STAT 354-01

      An introduction to probability theory and application. Fundamental probability concepts include: sample spaces, combinatorics, conditional probability, independence, random variables, probability distributions, expectation, variance, moment-generating functions, and limit theorems. Special course topics vary and may include: computer simulation, stochastic processes, and statistical inference. Prerequisite(s): MATH 137 or MATH 237

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      STAT 354-01 course materials and textbooks
      STAT 354-02 (10716)ProbabilityMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmTHEATR 203Instructor: Alexander Hanhart320

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with MATH 354-02 (10717)

      > Details for STAT 354-02

      An introduction to probability theory and application. Fundamental probability concepts include: sample spaces, combinatorics, conditional probability, independence, random variables, probability distributions, expectation, variance, moment-generating functions, and limit theorems. Special course topics vary and may include: computer simulation, stochastic processes, and statistical inference. Prerequisite(s): MATH 137 or MATH 237

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      STAT 354-02 course materials and textbooks
      STAT 451-01 (10718)Causal InferenceMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amTHEATR 204Instructor: Leslie Myint316

      Permission of instructor required

      > Details for STAT 451-01

      "Correlation does not imply causation." We’ve all heard this mantra, warding us away from reading too much into the association between murder rates and ice cream sales, between shoe size and reading ability, and the like. But this mantra leaves us wanting: how do we study causation? Questions of causation are essential when we try to understand the effects of new medical treatments, economic policies, or government programs. In this course, we’ll examine frameworks of thinking, statistical tools, and study designs that enable us to learn about the causal effects of interventions. Some specific topics include causal graphs, experimental studies, and quasi-experimental study designs. This course is a capstone course with a significant project component. Prerequisite(s): STAT 155

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      STAT 451-01 course materials and textbooks
      STAT 452-01 (10719)Correlated DataMeeting: M W 8:00 - 9:30 amOLRI 241Instructor: Brianna Heggeseth116

      Permission of instructor required

      > Details for STAT 452-01

      One of the most common assumptions made in Statistics is that observations are independent; however, there are many situations in which the data violate this assumption by design. In this class, we discuss advanced visualization and modeling approaches for when the data are correlated. Topics will include time series analysis, longitudinal data analysis, and spatial data analysis. Applications are drawn from across the disciplines. Prerequisite(s): STAT 155 and MATH 354/STAT 354

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      STAT 452-01 course materials and textbooks
      STAT 456-01 (10210)Projects in Data ScienceMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amOLRI 258Instructor: Bret Jackson620

      Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with COMP 456-01 (10209)

      > Details for STAT 456-01

      This third course in the data science curriculum is a capstone course that emphasizes team-based learning through open-ended data science projects. Working with a team throughout the course of the semester you will take on an interdisciplinary in-depth data science project and gain experience in developing and refining research questions, identifying and wrangling datasets, and clearly presenting results and conclusions. Mini-lectures by the instructor, guest speakers, and students will present advanced topics that supplement and support team-based learning. Counts as a capstone course for the Computer Science major and the Data Science major. Prerequisite(s): STAT 212  and STAT 253

      Distribution Requirements:

      Natural science and mathematics

      Course Materials:

      STAT 456-01 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Theater and Dance

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      THDA 112-01 (10720)Reading Plays: Asian and Asian American PlaywrightsMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amTHEATR 101Instructor: Randy Reyes114

      Cross-listed with AMST 102-01 (10721) and ASIA 194-01 (10722)

      > Details for THDA 112-01

      Reading Plays guides students in close readings of dramatic literature, in particular of plays that are typically left out of the traditional theatrical canon. In this edition, students will learn about the socio-historical context of each play, and in-class exercises will introduce them to the foundation of script analysis: they will examine the play’s given circumstances, dialogue, dramatic action, characters, and style. Students will read a new play every week; assignments include weekly in-class writing exercises and short critical papers. Some playwrights we may explore are: Ayad Akhtar, Jaclyn Backhaus, Christopher Chen, Mashuq Mushtaq Deen, Prince Gomolvilas, David Henry Hwang, Naomi Iizuka, Rajiv Joseph, Hansol Jung, Haruna Lee, Kimber Lee, Young Jean Lee, Qui Nguyen, Jiehae Park, Lloyd Suh, Kristina Wong, Lauren Yee, and Chay Yew.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism or U.S. Identities and Differences, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      THDA 112-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 120-01 (10723)ActingMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmTHEATR 120Instructor: Robert Rosen1114

      > Details for THDA 120-01

      This course introduces students to the art of acting. They will acquire fundamental acting skills and engage in  artistic experimentation. Students will learn how to create a repeatable score of psychophysical actions, script analysis, use the body/voice as the actor’s performing instrument; explore internal (memory, impulses, and so forth) and external (the other actors, the environment, objects, and so forth) stimuli as creative sources; rediscover of play and imagination; and develop work ethics and discipline. Students in this course will engage in physical activity and be required to spend 3-5 hours in rehearsals outside of scheduled class time.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      THDA 120-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 120-02 (10724)ActingMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmTHEATR 120Instructor: Robert Rosen514

      > Details for THDA 120-02

      This course introduces students to the art of acting. They will acquire fundamental acting skills and engage in  artistic experimentation. Students will learn how to create a repeatable score of psychophysical actions, script analysis, use the body/voice as the actor’s performing instrument; explore internal (memory, impulses, and so forth) and external (the other actors, the environment, objects, and so forth) stimuli as creative sources; rediscover of play and imagination; and develop work ethics and discipline. Students in this course will engage in physical activity and be required to spend 3-5 hours in rehearsals outside of scheduled class time.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      THDA 120-02 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 19-01 (10800)Introduction to DanceMeeting: M W F 10:50 - 11:50 amTHEATR 120Instructor: Marciano Silva Dos Santos818

      2 credits

      > Details for THDA 19-01

      This is a dance experience for students who have never taken a dance class before! Do you like moving to music? Do you like being part of a community? In this course you will develop coordination and experiment with rhythm. In warm ups, you will practice the principles of strengthening, stretching, and how to take care of your body and avoid injuries. We will improvise, play with composition, and try different styles to enjoy dance as a creative discipline. No experience necessary.

      Course Materials:

      THDA 19-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 200-F1 (10786)Dance and DesignMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amTHEATR 001Instructor: Mina Kinukawa1616

      First-Year Course only

      > Details for THDA 200-F1

      The unique qualities of dance as an art, a form of expression, and a mode of communication present fascinating opportunities for collaboration among dancers, choreographers, and designers. In this course, we will watch a variety of live and recorded dance performances with an emphasis on contemporary productions. We will learn to examine the relationship between dance and design elements (scenery, costume, light, sound, and projection design) through analysis and hands-on projects. Field trips on evenings and weekends.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      THDA 200-F1 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 215-01 (10725)Dance in the United States: A Cultural HistoryMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amHUM 102Instructor: Rae Fox-Charles914

      Cross-listed with HIST 294-05 (10785)

      > Details for THDA 215-01

      This course focuses on dance in the United States from the early 20th century to today with attention to how it has interacted with the nation’s cultural transformations. The historical study of dance as an embodied art form inevitably lends itself to an intersectional examination of how gender, race, ethnicity, and class experiences continually reflect and shape individual as well as collective identities. We will examine dance forms that have grown in and outside of concert halls, discuss seminal texts in dance and cultural studies, analyze dance videos and live performances, and write short papers that critically consider the relationship between the histories of American dance and culture over the last century.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      THDA 215-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 22-01 (10740)Beginning BalletMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmTHEATR 003Instructor: Jill LileClosed 014

      > Details for THDA 22-01

      This is a beginning ballet class designed for students who have never had ballet before. The goal is to demonstrate fundamental understanding and execution of basic ballet technique and to develop a foundation in movement that will carry over and support any other style of dance or physical practice. It will include barre and center work, across-the-floor combinations, and stretching and strengthening practices. Correct alignment, coordination, and body mechanics will be emphasized. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading. Every semester. (2 Credits)

      Course Materials:

      THDA 22-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 225-01 (10726)Technologies of Performance IIMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 am Instructor: Thomas Barrett412

      > Details for THDA 225-01

      This course will undertake the process of technical design;  the challenges and problems one must overcome in bringing a visual design to full realization.  We live in a performance world greatly influenced by film and spectacle; and these influences create great technical challenges. This course will expand and develop specific technical areas introduced in Technologies of Performance.  These areas will include:  stage rigging, structural design, metal fabrication and stage automation and control. Prerequisite(s): THDA 125

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1

      Course Materials:

      THDA 225-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 235-01 (10727)Fundamentals of ScenographyMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmTHEATR 006Instructor: Mina KinukawaClosed 012

      > Details for THDA 235-01

      Scenography is the creation of imagined spaces for performance. In this course we will study the fundamentals of scenography holistically, including scenic, lighting, costume, and sound design. Students will develop the ability to analyze and critique elements of performance design, articulate design ideas through written and verbal means, and complete a design project from textual analysis to tangible objects. This course is a prerequisite for intermediate and advanced design courses in Theater and Dance. Department provides course materials.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Quantitative Thinking Q1

      Course Materials:

      THDA 235-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 24-01 (10741)Beginning Contemporary DanceMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmTHEATR 120Instructor: Marciano Silva Dos Santos1018

      > Details for THDA 24-01

      This introductory level course explores the theory, technique, and terminology of modern dance as a performing art. Students engage fully with their bodies and minds as they deepen their strength, sense of rhythm, flexibility, and coordination. The course develops skills in inversions, floorwork, and balance based in a deepening awareness of alignment. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading.

      Course Materials:

      THDA 24-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 242-01 (10728)PlaywritingMeeting: W 7:00 - 10:00 pm Instructor: Jarek Pastor412

      Cross-listed with ENGL 285-01 (10729)

      > Details for THDA 242-01

      In this course, students engage in a series of playwriting exercises and read a wide variety of plays. They will read new and contemporary plays that employ different storytelling techniques (i.e., structure, character arcs, staging elements, etc.), in order to embrace the unlimited possibilities of theatricality, exemplifying why we write for the stage. Students will develop a "playwriting toolkit" as they explore their artistic interests. In-class exercises, prompts, small-group workshopping, and related readings will challenge each writer’s individual development. A mid-term check-in and in-class readings of final plays will allow students to hear their work out loud in a supportive public setting. Course may be taken twice for credit.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WC

      Course Materials:

      THDA 242-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 25-01 (10742)Beginning BreakingMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmTHEATR 003Instructor: Cheng Xiong1214

      > Details for THDA 25-01

      This course introduces students to fundamental characteristics, techniques, and principles of Breaking/Breakdance. The class will dive into the typical movements, histories and origins of this urban dance style. We are going into deep detail using theories and concepts that can help our minds, bodies and spirit connect further and deeper to the styles themselves. This class will allow your own identity and way of motion to be in the forefront. The course is physically rigorous, and emphasizes community building. We will utilize class time to learn and discuss the history of Breaking and its evolution by reading the book The Birth of Breaking by Serouj "Midus" Aprahamian. No experience required. All are welcome. May be repeated for credit.

      Course Materials:

      THDA 25-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 285-01 (10732)Costume DesignMeeting: M W F 9:40 - 10:40 amTHEATR 006Instructor: Mina KinukawaClosed 113

      > Details for THDA 285-01

      Students in this class will engage with costume design as both generative and collaborative artists. They will learn to conceptualize costumes for theater and dance productions drawing from a multitude of inspirations including the fine arts, costume/fashion history, music, literature, film, opera, and textiles. Class meetings will include discussions about readings and visual literacy, as well as hands-on exploration of a real-world design process including research, drawing and painting renderings, budgeting, fittings, and oral presentations of designs. Students will engage with both traditional and devised methods of developing characters and silhouettes to develop their unique vision for theatrical and dance productions. There will be 1-2 field trips to visit key resource locations for costume designers.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      THDA 285-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 292-01 (10733)Stage ManagementMeeting: M W F 12:00 - 1:00 pm Instructor: Wu Chen Khoo612

      Permission of instructor required; 2 credits

      > Details for THDA 292-01

      This course will introduce students to the multi-faceted responsibilities of the stage manager in theatrical stage productions. Through a combination of lectures, exercises, site visits and group discussion, students will learn the responsibilities and tools of managing any collaborative creative team. Each student will either Stage Manage or Assistant Stage Manage a THDA Production during the year. By the end of this course, students will have developed core proficiencies in note-taking, information management, giving written & verbal feedback, collaboration, leadership, communication, conflict intervention, decision making and team organization. Students receive 2 credits per semester, and 4 credits per year, but only after two consecutive semesters of enrollment. Every year. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite: Permission of Instructor only; Appropriate for First Years

      Course Materials:

      THDA 292-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 294-01 (10735)American Musical TheaterMeeting: M W F 3:30 - 4:30 pmTHEATR 101Instructor: Randy Reyes1414

      > Details for THDA 294-01

      In the United States there is a friendly and non-partisan divide between folks that love musicals and those who do not. If you are in the loving musicals camp, then this course is for you. And even if you don’t love it but are curious about why people love it--also for you. The course will explore different musical styles, structure, and themes -as it relates to the socio-political and cultural climate in which it was written and produced. We will read, discuss and analyze recordings, scripts, and live performances of great American musicals including SHOWBOAT, GUYS AND DOLLS, FLOWER DRUM SONG, CABARET, FIDDLER ON THE ROOF, LITTLE SHOP OF HORRORS, INTO THE WOODS, LION KING, IN THE HEIGHTS, FUN HOME, and HADESTOWN.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      THDA 294-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 294-03 (10050)Performance Art: History, Theory, and PraxisMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmARTCOM 102Instructor: Joanna Inglot
      kt shorb      		1120

      Cross-listed with ART 294-02 (10049)

      > Details for THDA 294-03

      This interdisciplinary course reimagines the study and practice of performance art as a bridge between disciplines and a dialogue between theory and practice, mind and body, history and experimentation. Students will gain a rare opportunity to think, move, and create across traditional disciplinary boundaries within fine arts, investigating how artists transform the body into a site of creation, resistance, and social critique—from early avant-garde disruptions to contemporary, cross-cultural performances in galleries and in public spaces. Students will engage with foundational texts on embodiment, spectatorship, and the politics of visibility alongside hands-on explorations of performance strategies by globally significant artists, ensuring that they learn not only about historical figures such as Marina Abramović, Joseph Beuys, and Carolee Schneemann, but also with cutting-edge global artists and experimental collectives in performance art today.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      Course Materials:

      THDA 294-03 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 32-01 (10743)Intermediate BalletMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmTHEATR 003Instructor: Jill Lile914

      Permission of instructor required

      > Details for THDA 32-01

      This is a beginning intermediate level ballet class for students who have acquired a foundation in the basic skills of classical ballet or have successfully completed THDA 51. Students will be expected to develop or expand their ballet technique while continuing to grow as dance artists. This class will consist of ballet barre and center work, simple pirouettes and the development of basic petit allegro steps. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading.

      Course Materials:

      THDA 32-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 34-01 (10744)Intermediate Contemporary DanceMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amTHEATR 120Instructor: Marciano Silva Dos Santos1218

      > Details for THDA 34-01

      Designed for intermediate to advanced dancers, the class invites you to explore the nuances of alignment, momentum, and articulation, while honing the relationship between movement and musicality. Each session is a dynamic blend of warm-ups, center exercises and dance phrases that challenge both body and mind. Building on the foundation of Beginning Contemporary Dance (or with permission of instructor), this course is a space to refine your expression, precision, and stamina. You will expand your technical skills to let your movement speak with clarity and power. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading. Every semester. Prerequisite(s): THDA 24 or permission of instructor.

      Course Materials:

      THDA 34-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 350-01 (10737)Directing for the StageMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 3:20 pmTHEATR 004Instructor: Randy ReyesClosed 010

      > Details for THDA 350-01

      This course prepares advanced theater students to direct public performances, and thus focuses on the acquisition of skills such as text analysis, dynamic storytelling, stage composition, and communication (with designers, actors, stage managers, and production team). Students will choose a scene from a play proposed by the instructor, research style and aesthetic, run auditions, cast the scene, facilitate a rehearsal process, and stage a live performance. They will also learn best collaborative practices, since the class unfolds in conversation with the Acting Approaches course (THDA360) and culminates in public performances at the James A. Williams Theater. Prerequisite(s): THDA 120 or THDA 235 or THDA 360, or permission of the instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WP

      Course Materials:

      THDA 350-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 42-01 (10745)Advanced BalletMeeting: M W F 3:30 - 4:45 pmTHEATR 003Instructor: Jill Lile1114

      Permission of instructor required

      > Details for THDA 42-01

      This is an advanced level ballet class for students who have achieved an advanced level of skill and artistry in classical ballet. Students will be expected to expand and develop their ballet technique while continuing to grow as dance artists. It will include barre work, pirouettes ending in all positions, and petit allegro with beats. Combinations will increase in complexity and students will be expected to pick up material quickly. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading. Prerequisite(s): Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor.

      Course Materials:

      THDA 42-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 490-01 (10738)Senior SeminarMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amTHEATR 101Instructor: kt shorb812

      > Details for THDA 490-01

      This is a reading and writing intensive course that engages theories from the fields of aesthetics and cultural studies to support each student’s in-depth research into a topic of their choice. Discussions examine how particular uses of the body, space, and narrative intersect to inform our experience of "performance," broadly defined, and engage the interplay between real and fictional in both artistic productions and performative social contexts. Students may pursue archival and library research, analysis of live performance, and analysis of documents of various kinds, including visual materials. Required for Theater and Dance majors, open to Dance, Theater, and Performance Design and Technologies minors.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      THDA 490-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 79-01 (10746)Performance Practice in Student ProjectsMeeting: MTWR 5:30 - 9:00 pmTHEATR 121Instructor: Mina Kinukawa5050

      Permission of instructor required; contact the Theater and Dance Department for audition and registration information

      > Details for THDA 79-01

      Students are involved in Theater and Dance Department productions as actors or dancers in student-led pieces. Performance Practice in Student Projects is the appropriate course when students will not work under direct supervision of a faculty member, but are part of a student director’s or student choreographer’s work in an Honors production or Dance Concert. Two credits awarded at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. S/N grading only. Every semester. (2 credits) Prerequisite(s): permission of instructor only.

      Course Materials:

      THDA 79-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 80-01 (10747)Performance PracticeMeeting: MTWR 5:30 - 9:00 pmTHEATR 121Instructor: Mina Kinukawa5050

      > Details for THDA 80-01

      Students are involved in production as actors or dancers, assistant choreographers or choreographers, assistant directors or directors, assistant designers or designers, dramaturgs and playwrights. Two credits awarded at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor required; please contact the Theater and Dance Department directly for audition and registration information

      Course Materials:

      THDA 80-01 course materials and textbooks
      THDA 81-01 (10748)Technical PracticeMeeting: MTWR 5:30 - 9:00 pmTHEATR 121Instructor: Mina Kinukawa
      Thomas Barrett
      Wu Chen Khoo      		5050

      > Details for THDA 81-01

      Students are involved in set, costume, lighting and sound engineering and construction, and running crew. Two credits awarded at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor required; please see instructor to be added to registration roster

      Course Materials:

      THDA 81-01 course materials and textbooks
      top of page

      Women's, Gender, and Sexuality Studies

      Num. / Sec. / CRNNameDaysTimeRoomInstructorOpen SeatsMax Enrollment
      WGSS 100-01 (10749)Introduction to Women’s, Gender, and Sexuality StudiesMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pm Instructor: Layla Zbinden1120

      > Details for WGSS 100-01

      This course is an introduction to the range and importance of 20th century as well as current feminist and queer theories and practices to our understanding of positive social change. It will analyze feminist and queer histories of resistance and alternatives to economic and political control, in the U.S. and elsewhere. The framework for the course is the intersection of gender and sexuality with race, class, nationality, and dis/ability; it will address such issues as economic marginalization, social movements, the institutions of family and marriage, migration and the role of the state/nation among others. Depending on the instructor, the course generally focuses on either LGBTQI studies or on transnational perspectives of these issues.

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 100-01 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 170-01 (10395)History of ChildhoodMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmHUM 400Instructor: Amy Sullivan615

      Cross-listed with HIST 170-01 (10394)

      > Details for WGSS 170-01

      This course examines the history of childhood and youth in the United States. The historical voices and perspectives of childhood that we study will pay close attention to the significance of race, ethnicity, class, gender, sexuality, time periods, and social settings. Some questions we will consider relevant to the study of history: Do children have agency? What is the role of children as subjects in history? How has childhood been socially and historically constructed? Why are children such galvanizing social and political symbols? How is identity shaped in childhood and what impact does this have on adult society at certain historical moments?

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WC

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 170-01 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 194-01 (10776)Gender Relations in IslamMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmMAIN 002Instructor: Ahoo Najafian1120

      Cross-listed with RELI 162-01 (10640)

      > Details for WGSS 194-01

      This course investigates the ways in which gender identities and relationships between “men” and “women” have been articulated, constructed, and refashioned throughout the Muslim World. Starting with problematizing fixed notions of gender and sexuality, we map prominent attitudes through visiting a diverse array of sources from the Qur’an, Sunna, and legal documents to historical and anthropological case studies, literature, and film from across the region. Exploring notions of femininity and masculinity, as well as attitudes towards homosexuality and transgendered populations, we witness their implications in case studies and stories, from a divorce court in Iran to a matriarchal utopia in India.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 194-01 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 258-01 (10160)Gender and Sexuality in ChinaMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmHUM 112Instructor: Xin Yang520

      Cross-listed with CHIN 258-01 (10158) and ASIA 258-01 (10159)

      > Details for WGSS 258-01

      How are masculinity and femininity defined and transformed in modern and contemporary Chinese culture? How is the culturally constructed gender related to a larger social context? Through a rigorous analysis of the content and structure of modern and contemporary novels and films, this course examines the literary representation of gender and sexuality and its relation to the tumultuous social transformations, and engages with a variety of themes including: May Fourth enlightenment, anti-Japanese war, Socialist construction, the Cultural Revolution, and the liberalization of the post-Mao era. This course seeks to help students develop critical views of Chinese society and culture from gendered perspective, and gain familiarity with major authors, genres, and literary movements. This course assumes no prior knowledge of China or Chinese, and all reading materials are in English.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 258-01 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 264-01 (10618)The Psychology of GenderMeeting: M W F 2:20 - 3:20 pmTHEATR 204Instructor: Melina Singh1432

      Cross-listed with PSYC 264-01 (10617)

      > Details for WGSS 264-01

      This course critically examines the psychology of women and gender from a feminist and social constructivist theoretical framework. Readings, assignments, and discussions foreground issues of identity, power, and inequality, as well as avenues for social change. We will explore how laws, institutional practices, gender norms, media representations, and commonly held attitudes and beliefs work together to reinforce women’s subordinate status and the psychological impacts of gender inequality on women, men, and gender nonconforming people. This course also utilizes an intersectional framework and interdisciplinary perspectives to highlight the ways that race, ethnicity, social class, sexuality, age, and other categories interact with gender at individual, interpersonal, and institutional levels. Counts as a UP3 course.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 264-01 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 294-01 (10750)Health and Power: Disability and Debility in a Globalized WorldMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amCARN 411Instructor: Layla Zbinden1820

      > Details for WGSS 294-01

      What is “health” in both a globalized world and a world shaped by legacies of colonialism, transatlantic enslavement, climate change, and forced migration? Is “healthy” a politically neutral and universally accessible classification? How do we understand disability - chronic or short term - as not just a biological experience, but a social one shaped by gender, sexuality, race, and empire? In this interdisciplinary class we will look at case studies to try to answer these questions. Some case studies include, but are not limited to, public health and labor migration between the US/Mexico border, medical racism and biological warfare, US imperialism in the Pacific Islands, the War on Gaza, detentions and quarantine in Guantanamo Bay, and South African apartheid. Through these examples, we will discuss the ways that health, medicine, and disability become used to uphold US/European power and white supremacy, and become the gauge for deciding who is worth the classification of “human” and who is deemed not worthy of life.

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 294-01 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 294-02 (10445)Gender and Japanese LanguageMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 110Instructor: Satoko Suzuki1520

      Cross-listed with JAPA 294-01 (10442), ASIA 294-01 (10443), and LING 294-01 (10444)

      > Details for WGSS 294-02

      When it comes to gender and English, people often think about third-person pronouns (they, she, he, ze, etc.), what others call you. In contrast, Japanese language speakers choose from a variety of first-person pronouns, what you call yourself. Some follow societal norms while others subvert them. This course examines how individuals and media creators make such linguistic choices (not just pronouns, but other expressions as well), and how these choices are fluid and based on such diverse factors as age, region, trend, rhetoric, and ideology. A very basic knowledge of Japanese is required, but all readings will be in English. Prerequisite: JAPA102 or permission of instructor.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 294-02 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 294-03 (10663)Women in ScienceMeeting: W 7:00 - 10:00 pmHUM 215Instructor: Maria Fedorova720

      Cross-listed with RUSS 294-01 (10661) and HIST 294-03 (10662)

      > Details for WGSS 294-03

      How did gender beliefs influence the development of modern science? How did women shape modern science? This course explores the intersection of gender and science from the early modern period to the late twentieth century. It focuses on the Western scientific tradition, with special attention to Russian and Soviet experiences. In this course, we will examine the following topics: early modern beliefs about nature, science, and gender; women’s participation in modern science; the development of modern scientific institutions; and biographies of women scientists.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 294-03 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 294-04 (10775)Before Homosexuality: Same-Sex Desire and Expression in Pre-Modern Islamicate WorldMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmTHEATR 201Instructor: Haci Gündüz420

      Cross-listed with CLAS 294-01 (10180), RELI 294-03 (10760) and HIST 294-04 (10782)

      > Details for WGSS 294-04

      This course explores how same-sex desire, eroticism, and gender variance were understood in pre-modern Islamicate societies before the emergence of modern sexual identities such as “homosexual” and “heterosexual.” Drawing on literary texts, legal treatises, medical writings, visual culture, and modern scholarship, students examine how desire was conceptualized in relation to gender, age, power, ethics, and social order. Rather than asking whether “gay people existed” in the past, the course asks how desire itself was structured, interpreted, and regulated and how modern categories of sexuality reshape our reading of pre-modern cultures. The course places Islamicate societies in global conversation with Greek, Christian, and modern European thought, emphasizing historical difference, cultural specificity, and the politics of interpretation.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 294-04 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 300-01 (10751)Worlds Upside Down: Revolutions in Theories and PracticesMeeting: T R 3:00 - 4:30 pmHUM 113Instructor: Melanie Yazzie1215

      > Details for WGSS 300-01

      Are we living in revolutionary times?  How do we know? This course takes a journey through the last 50 years of some large upheavals across the world-in language, politics, economics, culture, and media-to find some answers of how we are similar and different.  It uncovers how and why the struggles, in theories and practices, for power and representation have created the conditions in which we exist today. We travel through all kinds of ’post’ and ’neo’-liberalism, humanism, feminism, nationalism, and colonialism-and how they intertwine in our own lived experiences.  Some topics: protest against gender, sexual, race, economic, and linguistic bio-regimes, revolts of the colonized and marginalized, and resistant/ liberatory creations in art, technology, and media. Authors include Linda Tuhiwai Smith, Michel Foucault, Theresa Cha, Silvia Federici, Noam Chomsky, Ta-Nehisi Coates, bell hooks, Chela Sandoval, and Gayatri Spivak, among others.  At the end of the course, we will all know a little more about where we stand. People from all disciplines welcome. Prerequisite(s): Sophomore or junior standing or permission of instructor, and at least one course in the department (core course recommended, cross-listed accepted).

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 300-01 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 324-01 (10585)Women, Peace and SecurityMeeting: M W F 1:10 - 2:10 pmCARN 204Instructor: Wendy Weber520

      Cross-listed with POLI 324-01 (10584)

      > Details for WGSS 324-01

      In 2000, the United Nations Security Council adopted Resolution 1325 on Women, Peace and Security (WPS). This resolution acknowledged the inordinate impact of armed conflict on women and girls, as well as the crucial role that women can and do already play in conflict prevention, conflict resolution, peacekeeping and peacebuilding efforts. It urged states and other actors to increase the participation of women - and to incorporate gender perspectives - in all peace and security efforts. Since 2000, there have been nine additional resolutions that together have created the Women, Peace and Security agenda, a global framework for advancing gender equity in all areas of international peace and security. In this course we will explore feminist scholarship on armed conflict, peace and security leading up to the adoption of UNSCR 1325, as well as the 20 years of research, policy and practice on Women, Peace and Security. We will focus on the contributions and limitations of the WPS agenda as well as on new themes and issues that have emerged over time.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      Internationalism

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 324-01 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 354-01 (10502)Gender and MusicMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmMUSIC 219Instructor: Victoria Malawey012

      Permission of instructor required; MUSI 354-01 (10501)

      > Details for WGSS 354-01

      In this course, students will explore how gender is constructed in a variety of American and European musical styles and contexts, with an emphasis on popular genres. Learning objectives include for students to: (1) better understand the intersectional ways in which gender relates to and is informed by other aspects of identity formation, including class, race, and sexuality, (2) investigate issues that have affected women’s participation in musical life, such as musical canons, gendered musical discourse, and gender stereotypes, (3) explore contributions of trans and non-binary musicians, as well as issues that affect their musical lives, (4) interrogate constructions of gender, masculinity, and femininity as they relate to music, and (5) to develop reading comprehension, critical thinking skills, and argumentative writing skills.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Fine arts

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 354-01 course materials and textbooks

      WGSS 394-02 (10492)Critical and Creative Practices of Everyday LifeMeeting: T R 9:40 - 11:10 amHUM 314Instructor: Morgan Adamson516

      Cross-listed with MCST 388-01 (10491)

      > Details for WGSS 394-02

      This course explores how philosophers, creative writers, artists, and filmmakers have taken everyday life as a point of departure for critical reflection and creative practice. Grounded in cultural studies and feminist approaches to knowledge production, we will examine the everyday as a lens into a wide range of topics, including power, the body, race, gender, sexuality, capitalism, and colonialism. While surveying a wide range of approaches, the course will focus on the emerging genre of Autotheory, which blends critical theory with autobiography and memoir, leading to new insights and modes of resistance. In doing so, we will derive inspiration from contemporary writers, filmmakers, and artists who break with traditional approaches to academic writing and research by using the everyday as raw material. Students will have the opportunity to carry out a creative research project in the form of a written essay, film, or other multimedia form.

      Distribution Requirements:

      Humanities

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 394-02 course materials and textbooks
      WGSS 394-03 (10628)LGBTQ + HealthMeeting: T R 1:20 - 2:50 pmOLRI 270Instructor: Brian Rood316

      Cross-listed with PSYC 394-01 (10627)

      > Details for WGSS 394-03

      This is an advanced course on the health of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, and other sexually and gender diverse (LGBTQ+) people. The course is designed to engage students in a critical examination of health and healthcare disparities among LGBTQ+ populations, and evidence-based health models that explain the root causes and drivers of disparities. Students will also learn about and gain experience in behavioral interventions: how these are researched, developed, and implemented to target disparities, and promote the health and wellbeing of LGBTQ+ populations. Class time will consist of didactic instruction, large and small group discussions, documentary film viewings, application activities, and student presentations. Counts as a UP3 course. Prerequisite(s): PSYC 100; PSYC 201 or STAT 155; or permission of instructor

      Distribution Requirements:

      Social science

      General Education Requirements:

      U.S Identities and Differences

      Course Materials:

      WGSS 394-03 course materials and textbooks
